Section 1:
*EH1210.2*
Reprinted
February 20, 2026
ENGROSSED

DIGEST OF HB 1210 (Updated February 19, 2026 5:27 pm - DI 129)
Citations Affected: Numerous provisions throughout the Indiana
Code.
Synopsis: Department of local government finance. Requires a
municipal entity that hires or retains a municipal advisor to complete
a request for qualifications at least once every three years to select the
municipal advisor. Requires a municipal advisor to register with the
department of local government finance (DLGF). Provides for the
expiration of certain municipal entity contracts. Modifies the amount
of supplemental wagering tax that the treasurer of state is required to
pay to the riverboat operating in Gary and the distribution of wagering
(Continued next page)
Effective: Upon passage; July 1, 2018 (retroactive); January 1, 2024
(retroactive); January 1, 2025 (retroactive); May 10, 2025 (retroactive);
July 1, 2025 (retroactive); January 1, 2026 (retroactive); July 1, 2026;
July 1, 2027; January 1, 2028; June 30, 2028; July 1, 2028; January 1,

Section 2:
2029.
Snow, Lopez, Slager, Pryor
(SENATE SPONSORS — HOLDMAN, BALDWIN, MISHLER, ROGERS,
RANDOLPH LONNIE M)
January 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Ways and Means.
January 27, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass.
January 29, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
January 30, 2026, engrossed.
February 2, 2026, read third time, passed. Yeas 82, nays 1.
SENATE ACTION
February 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Tax and Fiscal Policy.
February 17, 2026, amended, reported favorably — Do Pass.
February 19, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
Digest Continued
tax revenue to the city of Gary. Provides that the Lake County
Convention Center Authority is established upon the substantial
completion of the convention and event center. Establishes an
alternative procedure for certain school corporations to issue refunding
bonds. Requires state agencies and political subdivisions to cooperate
with the state GIS officer in preparing a statewide base map. Eliminates
the requirement that the DLGF work with the office of technology or
another organization that is part of a state educational institution for
purposes of posting information on the Indiana transparency website
and submitting forms regarding data for local units. Allows a person to
satisfy any notice statute by publishing notice in specified forms of
media and modifies related provisions regarding notice by publication.
Provides that in assessing or reassessing land, the land shall be
assessed as agricultural land regardless of who owns the property or
who is liable for the property taxes. Specifies the burden of proof in
particular circumstances involving land previously assessed as
agricultural land. Amends and adds provisions regarding the
assessment of certain industrial facilities. Modifies procedures as to the
reporting of assessment values of real and personal property and parcel
level data. Changes the deadline for a county to submit to the DLGF
data regarding real property, personal property, and geographic
information system information. Requires the purchaser of a mobile
home to process the paperwork with the bureau of motor vehicles to
transfer the title into the purchaser's name within 90 days of the sale.
Specifies eligibility for certain townships to petition for an increase to
the maximum property tax levy for the firefighting and emergency
services fund. Makes procedural changes for civil taxing units not
subject to levy limits. Provides that certain local property tax credits
result in a reduction of property tax collections in a political
subdivision in which such a credit is applied. Specifies the procedures
for the submission of certain forms and related allocation amounts with
regard to various allocation areas. Amends various tax increment
financing allocation area provisions, including provisions to redefine
"residential property" and to require the original owner of certain
nonowner-occupied residential property in an allocation area to enter
into a written agreement with the appropriate entity to pay the property
taxes for the portion of outstanding bonds until the bonds are retired.
Changes reporting requirements by governing bodies to the DLGF
regarding guaranteed savings contracts and energy efficient programs
used by school corporations. Provides that the property tax rate for the
levy imposed for the replacement of fire protection territory equipment
is considered part of the maximum permissible ad valorem property tax
levy and may not exceed $0.0333 per $100 of assessed value. Extends
a temporary increase in the capitalization rate percentage under the
statewide agricultural land base rate determination. Requires the DLGF
to annually publish on the Indiana Register the adjusted cost estimate
threshold for certain local public work projects. Provides a real and
personal property tax exemption for Indiana nonprofit senior living
communities beginning with property taxes that are first due and
payable in 2027. Adds the the Indiana Historical Society, Inc. to a list
of organizations exempt from property taxation. Allows certain
taxpayers to retroactively file a property tax exemption application.
Increases the number of model residences and residences in inventory
from three to seven that may be claimed for purposes of those property
tax deductions. Requires an individual to reside on the real property,
mobile home, or manufactured home to be eligible for the over 65
property tax credit. Increases the property tax deduction for a veteran
who is totally disabled to 100% of the assessed value of the individual's
real property (instead of $14,000). Expires property tax deductions for
certain veterans, and instead provides a property tax liability credit.
Restores the property tax deduction available to a surviving spouse of
a World War I veteran that was limited by SEA 1-2025. Provides, if a
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344
Digest Continued
taxpayer claims the homestead deduction for property that is not
eligible for the deduction, that the taxpayer shall (instead of may) be
liable for any additional taxes that would have been due on homestead
property plus a civil penalty. Requires the county auditor to include in
a notice of tax due a 10% fine as a penalty for claiming the homestead
deduction falsely, which is in addition to all other penalties for which
the taxpayer is liable. Allows the executive of Miami Township in Cass
County to submit a petition to the DLGF requesting an increase in the
township's maximum permissible ad valorem property tax levy for
property taxes first due and payable in 2027. Requires the Hancock
County fiscal body to adopt a resolution to allow a one time transfer of
money from the library property tax replacement fund. Provides that for
purposes of fixing and reviewing budgets, tax rates, and tax levies,
before a county auditor makes an amendment, the county auditor must
provide written notice to the county fiscal body, the DLGF, and the
fiscal officers of the affected taxing units. Provides that the DLGF may
not approve the budget for a political subdivision until an attestation
statement concerning the uploading of contracts is submitted. Requires
certain qualified data center users to enter into an agreement with local
officials before the qualified data center user may use a specific
transaction award certificate. Adds a provision regarding repayment in
the redevelopment tax credit. Specifies eligibility and procedures for
a health reimbursement arrangement income tax credit. Moves the
effective date for the local income tax changes enacted in SEA 1-2025
from 2028 to 2029. Makes corresponding changes to move the
expiration date regarding a county with a single voting bloc enacted in
HEA 1142-2025. Specifies procedures for the imposition of local
income taxes and distribution of local income tax revenue. Removes
provisions enacted in SEA 1-2025 providing for the expiration of local
income tax expenditure rates for counties or municipalities that fail to
adopt an ordinance to renew an existing expenditure tax rate. Provides
that the county may determine an allocation method for revenue raised
from a tax rate for fire protection or emergency medical services.
Provides that the county and certain township fire departments must
receive an allocation of revenue raised from a tax rate for fire
protection or emergency medical services. Provides a formula for the
distribution of revenue from the local income tax rate imposed by a
county for certain small cities and towns. Specifies procedures for
determining population for purposes of a municipal local income tax
rate. Allows each county to establish a local strategic taskforce to
negotiate and determine certain maximum local income tax rates.
Allows the Marshall County jail fund to be used for costs otherwise
incurred for the operation of the county jail. Provides that distributions
of certain excise tax revenue to a taxing unit may be deposited in any
fund maintained by the taxing unit and may be used for any purpose
allowed by law. Makes numerous changes and additions to the local
innkeeper's tax and food and beverage tax statutes, including new food
and beverage tax authorizations, modifications to distributions of
innkeeper's tax revenue, and revisions to the composition of various
innkeeper's tax boards and commissions. Replaces the definitions of
"manufactured home" and "mobile home" throughout the Indiana Code
with a singular definition. Subject to an exception, limits voting
eligibilty in a homeowners association to members of the homeowners
association who use their property as a homestead as to certain matters.
With certain exceptions, prohibits a unit from adopting or enforcing an
ordinance, resolution, regulation, policy, or rule that prohibits or
restricts an owner of a privately owned residential property from using
the property as a rental property. Retroactively amends the definition
of "short term rental" to specify that certain private, owner occupied
businesses are excluded from that definition. Requires a person
appointed to a fire protection district board of trustees to reside in the
fire protection district. Sets forth an alternative procedure that may be
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344
Digest Continued
used to appoint the board of trustees of certain fire protection districts.
Provides that a fire protection district established after December 31,
2025, may not impose a tax rate within the district that exceeds $0.40
per $100 of assessed valuation. Revises a provision of the municipal
Barrett Law concerning deferred installments. Amends provisions in
the drainage law as to bidding on certain projects and the term of loans.
Authorizes the budget agency, subject to budget committee review, to
augment the state agency contingency fund appropriations in HEA
1001-2025 through July 1, 2027, in an amount not to exceed
$40,000,000 for Indiana office of technology contracts, in addition to
the uses for the appropriation authorized in HEA 1001-2025.
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344
Reprinted
February 20, 2026
Second Regular Session of the 124th General Assembly (2026)
PRINTING CODE. Amendments: Whenever an existing statute (or a section of the Indiana
Constitution) is being amended, the text of the existing provision will appear in this style type,
additions will appear in this style type, and deletions will appear in this style type.
Additions: Whenever a new statutory provision is being enacted (or a new constitutional
provision adopted), the text of the new provision will appear in this style type. Also, the
word NEW will appear in that style type in the introductory clause of each SECTION that adds
a new provision to the Indiana Code or the Indiana Constitution.
Conflict reconciliation: Text in a statute in this style type or this style type reconciles conflicts
between statutes enacted by the 2025 Regular Session of the General Assembly.

A BILL FOR AN ACT to amend the Indiana Code concerning state
and local administration and to make an appropriation.

3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 18. (a) If the election division determines that the
4 proposed precinct establishment order would comply with this chapter,

11 executive.

15 (1) The name of each existing precinct whose boundaries would

1 SECTION 5. IC 3-12-3.5-8, AS AMENDED BY P.L.210-2018,
2 SECTION 9, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8. (a) As used in this section, "audit threshold
4 number" refers to the following number:
5 (1) One (1), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
6 subsection (c), is not more than twenty (20).
7 (2) Two (2), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
8 subsection (c), is:
9 (A) more than twenty (20); but
10 (B) not more than forty (40).
11 (3) Three (3), if the total number of votes cast, as determined
12 under subsection (c), is:
13 (A) more than forty (40); but
14 (B) not more than sixty (60).
15 (4) Four (4), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
16 subsection (c), is:
17 (A) more than sixty (60); but
18 (B) not more than eighty (80).
19 (5) Five percent (5%) of the total number of votes cast, rounded
20 up to the nearest whole number, if the total number of votes cast,
21 as determined under subsection (c), is:
22 (A) more than eighty (80); but
23 (B) not more than five hundred (500).
24 (6) Twenty-five (25), if the total number of votes cast, as
25 determined under subsection (c) is more than five hundred (500).
26 (b) As used in this section, "judge" refers only to the judge who is
27 a member of a political party other than the political party of the
28 inspector.
29 (c) After each electronic voting system has been secured and the
30 paper vote total printouts obtained, the inspector and judge shall record
31 the total number of:
32 (1) votes cast on all electronic voting systems located within the
33 precinct; and
34 (2) voters who have received a ballot by signing in at the polls
35 according to the poll lists for each precinct;
36 to determine if the total number of votes cast on the electronic voting
37 systems differs from the number of voters shown to have received a
38 ballot at the polls according to the poll lists.

18 SECTION 6. IC 4-23-7.3-5.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
19 CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
20 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 5.5. As used in this chapter,
21 "governmental boundary units" includes:
22 (1) the geographic boundaries of a political subdivision;
23 (2) the geographic boundaries of a taxing district (as defined
24 by IC 6-1.1-1-20); and
25 (3) any geographic boundaries related to the operation of the
26 statewide 911 system under IC 36-8-16.7.
27 SECTION 7. IC 4-23-7.3-16, AS AMENDED BY P.L.134-2021,
28 SECTION 8, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
29 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 16. With money from the fund, the state GIS
30 officer, through the data center, the IGIC, and the other organizations,
31 shall do the following:
32 (1) Ensure that there are adequate depositories of all GIS data and
33 framework data obtained by a state agency.
34 (2) Acquire, publish, store, and distribute GIS data and
35 framework data through the computer gateway administered
36 under IC 4-13.1-2-2(a)(6) by the office of technology and through
37 the state data center. The state GIS officer may also provide
38 access through the IGIC and other entities as directed by the state
39 GIS officer.
40 (3) Integrate GIS data and framework data developed and
41 maintained by state agencies and political subdivisions into the
42 statewide base map. State agencies and political subdivisions
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
8
1 shall cooperate and participate as requested by the state GIS
2 officer to carry out this subdivision.
3 (4) Maintain a state historical archive of GIS data, framework
4 data, and electronic maps.
5 (5) Except as otherwise provided in this chapter, provide public
6 access to GIS data and framework data in locations throughout
7 Indiana.
8 (6) Provide assistance to state agencies and political subdivisions
9 regarding public access to GIS data and framework data so that
10 information is available to the public while confidentiality is
11 protected for certain data from electronic maps.
12 (7) Develop and maintain statewide framework data layers
13 associated with a statewide base map or electronic map.
14 (8) Publish and distribute the state GIS data standards and the
15 statewide data integration plan adopted under section 14(2) of this
16 chapter.
17 (9) Subject to section 20 of this chapter, make GIS data,
18 framework data, and electronic maps available for use by the
19 Indiana Business Research Center.
20 SECTION 8. IC 4-23-7.3-20, AS ADDED BY P.L.198-2007,
21 SECTION 2, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
22 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 20. (a) Except as provided in subsections (b), (c),
23 and (d), a political subdivision maintains the right to control the sale,
24 exchange, and distribution of any GIS data or framework data provided
25 by the political subdivision to the state through a data exchange
26 agreement entered into under this chapter.
27 (b) A political subdivision may agree, through a provision in a data
28 exchange agreement, to allow the sale, exchange, or distribution of GIS
29 data or framework data provided to the state.
30 (c) Subsection (a) does not apply to data that is otherwise required
31 by state or federal law to be provided by a political subdivision to the
32 state or federal government.
33 (d) As a condition in a data exchange agreement for providing state
34 GIS data or framework data to a political subdivision, The state GIS
35 officer may require the political subdivision to follow the state GIS data
36 standards and the statewide data integration plan when the political
37 subdivision makes use of the GIS data or framework data as provided

10 (c) This subsection does not apply to tax revenue remitted by an
11 inland casino operating in Vigo County. For each city and county
12 receiving money under subsection (a)(2), the state comptroller shall

15 determined is the base year revenue for the city or county. The state

27 of each year, the state comptroller shall distribute the wagering taxes
28 set aside for revenue sharing under subsection (a)(1) to the county
29 treasurer of each county that does not have a riverboat according to the
30 ratio that the county's population bears to the total population of the
31 counties that do not have a riverboat. Except as provided in subsection
32 (g), the county auditor shall distribute the money received by the
33 county under this subsection as follows:
34 (1) To each city located in the county according to the ratio the
35 city's population bears to the total population of the county.
36 (2) To each town located in the county according to the ratio the
37 town's population bears to the total population of the county.
38 (3) After the distributions required in subdivisions (1) and (2) are
39 made, the remainder shall be retained by the county.

22
1 a particular year (a property tax reduction under this subdivision

13 SECTION 14. IC 5-1-5-2, AS AMENDED BY P.L.229-2011,
14 SECTION 62, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
15 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2. (a) The governing body of any issuing body
16 may by ordinance provide for the issuance of bonds to refund
17 outstanding bonds issued at any time by such issuing body or its
18 predecessor, and to pay redemption premiums and costs of refunding
19 to effect a saving to the issuing body. Issuance of bonds to refund
20 outstanding bonds may also be made in order to pay or discharge all or
21 any part of such outstanding series or issue of bond, including any
22 interest thereon, in arrears or about to become due and for which
23 sufficient funds are not available or to modify restrictive covenants in
24 outstanding bonds impeding additional financing. To determine
25 whether or not a savings will be effected, consideration shall be given
26 to the estimated or known interest payable to the fixed maturities of the
27 refunding bonds, the interest payable on the bonds to be refunded, the
28 costs of issuance of the refunding bonds, including any sale discount,
29 the redemption premiums, if any, to be paid, and the probable earned
30 income from the investment of the refunding bond proceeds pending
31 redemption of the bonds to be refunded.
32 (b) The provisions of subsection (a) requiring a savings to be
33 effected do not apply to:
34 (1) the issuance of bonds to refund previously issued refunding
35 bonds, if the statute under which the refunding bonds are issued
36 expressly exempts such an issue from this savings requirement;
37 or
38 (2) the issuance of refunding bonds by a school corporation that
39 is an eligible school corporation under section 2.5 or 2.6 of this
40 chapter.
41 SECTION 15. IC 5-1-5-2.6 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
42 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
28
1 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2.6. (a) This section applies as an
2 alternative to section 2.5 of this chapter.
3 (b) As used in this section, "eligible school corporation" means
4 a school corporation (as defined in IC 36-1-2-17) that satisfies all
5 the conditions required by this section.
6 (c) As used in this section, "increment" means the annual
7 difference between:
8 (1) the annual debt service payment for the bonds proposed to
9 be retired or refunded; and
10 (2) the annual debt service payment for the proposed
11 refunding bonds;
12 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or refunded
13 would have been outstanding.
14 (d) In order for a school corporation to be an eligible school
15 corporation under this section, the school corporation must
16 determine that:
17 (1) the percentage computed under subsection (e) for the
18 school corporation is at least sixty percent (60%); and
19 (2) application of this section will not reduce revenue to other
20 local units in the same county;
21 regarding the year for which the latest certified levies have been
22 determined.
23 (e) A school corporation shall compute its percentage as follows:
24 (1) Compute the amount of credits granted under
25 IC 6-1.1-20.6 against the school corporation's levy for the
26 school corporation's operations fund (IC 20-46-8).
27 (2) Compute the school corporation's levy for the school
28 corporation's operations fund.
29 (3) Divide the amount computed under subdivision (1) by the
30 amount computed under subdivision (2) and express it as a
31 percentage.
32 (f) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
33 corporation under this section must submit a written request for
34 a certification by the department of local government finance that
35 the computation of the school corporation's percentage computed
36 under subsection (d)(1) and school corporation's determination
37 under subsection (d)(2) are correct. The department of local
38 government finance shall, not later than thirty (30) business days
39 after the date the department receives the school corporation's
40 request, make its determination and, if correct, certify that the
41 percentage computed under subsection (d)(1) and the
42 determination under subsection (d)(2) for the school corporation
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
29
1 meets the definition of an eligible school corporation.
2 (g) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
3 corporation under this section must, and notwithstanding any
4 other law, adopt a resolution that sets forth the following:
5 (1) The determinations made under subsection (d)(1) and
6 (d)(2), including the department of local government finance's
7 certification of the percentage computed under subsection (e)
8 and determination under subsection (d)(2).
9 (2) A determination providing for the:
10 (A) issuance of bonds to refund bonds or leases issued by
11 or on behalf of the school corporation before July 1, 2025;
12 and
13 (B) payment of redemption premiums and the costs of the
14 refunding.
15 (3) With respect to the refunding bonds, the following:
16 (A) The maximum principal amount.
17 (B) The maximum interest rate.
18 (C) The annual lease or debt service payment.
19 (D) The final maturity date.
20 (E) The estimated amount of the increment that will occur
21 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or
22 refunded by the issuance of refunding bonds would have
23 been outstanding.
24 (F) A finding that the annual debt service or lease payment
25 on the refunding bonds will not increase the annual debt
26 service or lease payment above the annual debt service or
27 lease payment approved by the school corporation for the
28 original project.
29 If the governing body adopts a resolution under this section, the
30 governing body must publish notice of the adoption of the
31 resolution as required by IC 5-3-1.
32 (h) An eligible school corporation may issue refunding bonds as
33 permitted by this section. In addition, an eligible school
34 corporation may extend the repayment period beyond the
35 repayment period for the bonds that are being retired or refunded
36 by the issuance of refunding bonds. However, the repayment
37 period may be extended only once for a particular bond, and the
38 extension may not exceed ten (10) years after the latest maturity
39 date for any of the bonds being retired or refunded by the eligible
40 school corporation under this section.

10 SECTION 19. IC 5-1-14-19 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
11 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JULY
12 1, 2026]: Sec. 19. (a) This section applies to a contract between a
13 municipal entity and a municipal advisor entered into, renewed, or
14 amended after June 30, 2026.
15 (b) As used in this section, "municipal advisor" means an entity
16 defined as a municipal advisor under Section 15B of the Securities
17 Exchange Act.
18 (c) A municipal advisor must meet the following criteria:
19 (1) A municipal advisor employing more than one (1)
20 employee must employ at least one (1) individual who meets
21 both of the following criteria:
22 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
23 under applicable federal law and regulations.
24 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
25 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
26 54), or any successor examination recognized by the
27 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
28 (2) A municipal advisor employing only one (1) employee
29 must employ an individual who meets both of the following
30 criteria:
31 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
32 under applicable federal law and regulations.
33 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
34 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
35 50), or any successor examination recognized by the
36 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
37 (d) As used in this section, "municipal entity" refers to:
38 (1) a state agency (as defined in IC 4-1-10-2);
39 (2) a county;
40 (3) a township;
41 (4) a city;
42 (5) a town;
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
33
1 (6) a school corporation;
2 (7) a special taxing district;
3 (8) an instrumentality of an entity listed in subdivisions (1)
4 through (7); and
5 (9) any other entity required to sell bonds pursuant to
6 IC 5-1-11.
7 (e) An entity that provides municipal advisory services,
8 including but not limited to financial planning, debt issuance, and
9 budget management advisory services, to:
10 (1) a state agency;
11 (2) a political subdivision (as defined in IC 36-1-2-13); or
12 (3) a municipal entity located in Indiana;
13 must comply with the municipal advisor qualification requirements
14 under subsection (c), starting January 1, 2027. An employee of a
15 state agency or employee of a political subdivision (as defined in
16 IC 36-1-2-13) is not required to meet the qualifications under
17 subsection (c).
18 (f) If a municipal entity hires or retains a municipal advisor, the
19 municipal entity shall complete a request for qualifications at least
20 once every three (3) years to select the municipal advisor. The
21 request for qualifications must include a scope of services and an
22 evaluation criteria outline. The request for qualifications shall be
23 completed before July 1 each year it is completed.
24 (g) Municipal entity contracts executed on or before July 1,
25 2023, with a term of three (3) years or more, shall expire on the
26 earlier of the contract expiration date or January 1, 2027.
27 (h) The municipal entity shall publish a contract entered into
28 with a municipal advisor in a prominent location on the municipal
29 entity's website and on the department of local government
30 finance's computer gateway.
31 (i) Once selected for a municipal entity's contract, the municipal
32 advisor shall register with the department of local government
33 finance. The department of local government finance shall give the
34 municipal advisor access to the municipal entity's gateway portal
35 upon receipt of their registration. If the municipal advisor does not
36 register with the department of local government finance, the
37 department of local government finance may issue a fine of ten
38 thousand dollars ($10,000) to the municipal advisor.

7 governor concerning the appointment of a director under this
8 subdivision.
9 (b) The commission shall be governed by the board. The directors
10 may not be elected public officials of the state or any political
11 subdivision. Except for the budget director, the directors first appointed
12 continue in office for terms expiring on July 1, 2014, July 1, 2015, July
13 1, 2016, and July 1, 2017, and until their respective successors are duly
14 appointed and qualified.
15 (c) Except for the budget director, the term of any director first
16 appointed must be designated by the governor. If a vacancy occurs on
17 the board, the governor shall fill the vacancy by appointing a new
18 director. The successor of each such director is appointed for a term of
19 four (4) years, except that any person appointed to fill a vacancy is
20 appointed to serve only for the unexpired term and until a successor is
21 duly appointed and qualified. A director is eligible for reappointment.
22 (d) The directors shall hold an initial organizational meeting within
23 thirty (30) days after the board's appointment and after public notice
24 given by the budget director in accordance with IC 5-3-1-4.
25 IC 5-3-1-1.5. As soon as practicable after January 15 of each year, the
26 board shall hold its annual organizational meeting. The board shall
27 elect one (1) of the directors as vice chair and another director as
28 secretary-treasurer to perform the duties of those offices. These officers
29 serve from the date of their election and until their successors are
30 elected and qualified. Special meetings may be called by the chair or
31 any two (2) directors of the board.
32 (e) Three (3) directors constitute a quorum of the board, and the
33 affirmative vote of at least three (3) directors is necessary for any
34 official action taken by the board. A vacancy in the membership of the
35 board does not impair the rights of a quorum to exercise all the rights
36 and perform all the duties of the board.
37 (f) Except for the budget director, the directors are entitled to
38 reimbursement for traveling expenses and other expenses actually
39 incurred in connection with their duties as provided by law. Directors
40 are not entitled to the salary per diem provided by IC 4-10-11-2.1(b) or
41 any other compensation while performing their duties.
42 (g) All expenses incurred in carrying out the provisions of this
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
35
1 chapter shall be payable solely from funds provided under this chapter
2 or from the proceeds of bonds issued by the authority under this
3 chapter, and no liability or obligation shall be incurred by the
4 commission or the authority under this chapter beyond the extent to
5 which money shall have been provided under the authority of this
6 chapter.
7 (h) The board:
8 (1) is responsible for implementing the powers and duties of the
9 commission under this chapter;
10 (2) may adopt bylaws for the regulation of the affairs of the board,
11 the conduct of the business of the commission, and the
12 safeguarding of the funds and property entrusted to the
13 commission; and
14 (3) shall, without complying with IC 4-22-2, adopt the code of
15 ethics specified in executive order 05-12 for its members and
16 employees.

1 not later than twenty-one (21) days before election day.

33 SECTION 25. IC 5-3-1-4 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY 1,
34 2026]. Sec. 4. (a) Whenever officers of a political subdivision are
35 required to publish a notice affecting the political subdivision, they
36 shall publish the notice in two (2) newspapers published in the political
37 subdivision.
38 (b) This subsection applies to notices published by county officers.
39 If there is only one (1) newspaper published in the county, then
40 publication in that newspaper alone is sufficient.
41 (c) This subsection applies to notices published by city, town, or
42 school corporation officers. If there is only one (1) newspaper
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
42
1 published in the municipality or school corporation, then publication
2 in that newspaper alone is sufficient. If no newspaper is published in
3 the municipality or school corporation, then publication of the notice
4 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
5 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
6 school corporation.
7 (2) A newspaper published in the county in which the
8 municipality or school corporation is located and that circulates
9 within the municipality or school corporation.
10 (d) This subsection applies to notices published by officers of
11 political subdivisions not covered by subsection (a) or (b). If there is
12 only one (1) newspaper published in the political subdivision, then the
13 notice shall be published in that newspaper. If no newspaper is
14 published in the political subdivision, then publication of the notice
15 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
16 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
17 school corporation.
18 (2) A newspaper published in the county and that circulates
19 within the political subdivision.
20 (e) This subsection applies to a political subdivision, including a
21 city, town, or school corporation. Notwithstanding any other law, if a
22 political subdivision has territory in more than one (1) county, public
23 notices that are required by law or ordered to be published must be
24 given as follows:
25 (1) By publication in two (2) newspapers published within the
26 boundaries of the political subdivision.
27 (2) If only one (1) newspaper is published within the boundaries
28 of the political subdivision, by publication of the notice in that
29 newspaper and in one (1) of the following:
30 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
31 subdivision.
32 (B) In another newspaper:
33 (i) published in any county in which the political subdivision
34 extends; and
35 (ii) that has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
36 (3) If no newspaper is published within the boundaries of the
37 political subdivision, by publishing the notice in two (2)
38 publications, consisting of either or both of the following:
39 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
40 subdivision.
41 (B) A newspaper that:
42 (i) is published in any counties into which the political
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
43
1 subdivision extends; and
2 (ii) has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
3 (4) If only one (1) newspaper is published in any of the counties
4 into which the political subdivision extends, by publication of the
5 notice in one (1) of the following:
6 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
7 subdivision.
8 (B) The newspaper published in the county if the newspaper
9 circulates within the political subdivision.
10 (f) A political subdivision may, in its discretion, publish public
11 notices in a qualified publication or additional newspapers or locality
12 newspapers to provide supplementary notification to the public. The
13 cost of publishing supplementary notification is a proper expenditure
14 of the political subdivision.

2 SECTION 5, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
3 JULY 1, 2025 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 11. (a) As used in this section,
4 "tax increment financing allocation area" means any area authorized by
5 statute in which ad valorem property taxes are allocated, including the
6 following:
7 (1) IC 6-1.1-39 (economic development districts).
8 (2) IC 8-22-3.5 (airport development zones).
9 (3) IC 36-7-14 (redevelopment of areas needing redevelopment
10 generally).
11 (4) IC 36-7-15.1 (redevelopment of areas in Marion County).
12 (5) IC 36-7-30 (reuse of federal military bases).

14 bases).

16 (8) IC 36-7-32.5 (innovation development districts).
17 (9) IC 36-7.5-4.5 (rail transit development districts).
18 (b) The department shall, in each year beginning after December 31,
19 2025, 2026, and ending before January 1, 2034, adjust the base
20 assessed value of each tax increment financing allocation area to
21 neutralize the effect of the changing tax rates resulting year to year
22 from the homestead deduction under IC 6-1.1-12-37(c)(2) and
23 IC 6-1.1-12-37.5(c) and the deduction for eligible property under
24 IC 6-1.1-12-47. It is the intent of the general assembly that an increase
25 in revenue from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes
26 accrue only to the base assessed value and not to the tax increment
27 financing allocation area. However, in the case of a decrease in revenue
28 from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes, the department
29 may neutralize the change under this subsection in a positive manner
30 with regard to the tax increment financing allocation area to protect the
31 ability to pay bonds based on incremental revenue, if the tax increment
32 financing allocation area demonstrates to the department that an
33 adjustment is needed before the department calculates a positive
34 neutralization adjustment.

17 political subdivision in the county subject to IC 6-1.1-17-16. The
18 electronic notice must include a written statement acknowledging
19 noncompliance and detail the reasons why the statutory deadline
20 provided in subsection (b) was not met.
21 (d) The department of local government finance shall, before
22 February 2, 2027, and before February 2 of each year thereafter,
23 submit a report of the counties that failed to meet the statutory
24 deadline set forth in subsection (b) to the legislative services agency
25 for distribution to the members of the legislative council. The
26 report must be in an electronic format under IC 5-14-6.

25 requirement for an annual adjustment for each assessment date, the

27 of each year, determine the base rate using the methodology reflected
28 in Table 2-18 of Book 1, Chapter 2 of the department of local
29 government finance's Real Property Assessment Guidelines (as in
30 effect on January 1, 2005), except that the department shall adjust the
31 methodology as follows:
32 (1) Use a six (6) year rolling average adjusted under subdivision
33 (3) instead of a four (4) year rolling average.
34 (2) Use the data from the six (6) most recent years preceding the
35 year in which the assessment date occurs for which data is
36 available, before one (1) of those six (6) years is eliminated under
37 subdivision (3) when determining the rolling average.
38 (3) Eliminate in the calculation of the rolling average the year
39 among the six (6) years for which the highest market value in use
40 of agricultural land is determined.
41 (4) After determining a preliminary base rate that would apply for
42 the assessment date without applying the adjustment under this
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
48
1 subdivision, the department of local government finance shall
2 adjust the preliminary base rate as follows:
3 (A) If the preliminary base rate for the assessment date would
4 be at least ten percent (10%) greater than the final base rate
5 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization
6 rate of:
7 (i) for purposes of determining the preliminary base rate for
8 the January 1, 2025, and the January 1, 2026, and January
9 1, 2027, assessment dates, nine percent (9%); and
10 (ii) for purposes of determining the preliminary base rate for
11 assessment dates before January 1, 2025, and for assessment
12 dates after December 31, 2026, 2027, eight percent (8%);
13 shall be used to determine the final base rate.
14 (B) If the preliminary base rate for the assessment date would
15 be at least ten percent (10%) less than the final base rate
16 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization
17 rate of six percent (6%) shall be used to determine the final
18 base rate.
19 (C) If neither clause (A) nor clause (B) applies, a capitalization
20 rate of seven percent (7%) shall be used to determine the final
21 base rate.
22 (D) In the case of a market value in use for a year that is used
23 in the calculation of the six (6) year rolling average under
24 subdivision (1) for purposes of determining the base rate for
25 the assessment date:

28 for the calculation of the base rate for the assessment date;

30 (ii) the market value in use recalculated under item (i) shall
31 be used in the calculation of the six (6) year rolling average
32 under subdivision (1).

5 when it is devoted to agricultural use regardless of:
6 (1) who owns the land; or
7 (2) who the person or entity is that is liable for property taxes
8 due on the land under IC 6-1.1-2-4.

10 considered devoted to agricultural use, any:
11 (1) land enrolled in:
12 (A) a farm program of the state or of the United States
13 Department of Agriculture, including all acreage within an
14 assigned farm number;
15 (A) (B) a land conservation or reserve program administered
16 by the United States Department of Agriculture;
17 (B) (C) a land conservation program administered by the
18 United States Department of Agriculture's Farm Service
19 Agency; or
20 (C) (D) a conservation reserve program or agricultural
21 easement program administered by the United States
22 Department of Agriculture's National Natural Resources
23 Conservation Service;
24 (2) land enrolled in the department of natural resources' classified
25 forest and wildlands program (or any similar or successor
26 program);
27 (3) land classified in the category of other agriculture use, as
28 provided in the department of local government finance's real
29 property assessment guidelines; or
30 (4) land devoted to the harvesting of hardwood timber;
31 is considered to be devoted to agricultural use. Agricultural use for
32 purposes of this section includes but is not limited to the uses included
33 in the definition of "agricultural use" in IC 36-7-4-616(b), such as the
34 production of livestock or livestock products, commercial aquaculture,
35 equine or equine products, land designated as a conservation reserve
36 plan, pastureland, poultry or poultry products, horticultural or nursery
37 stock, fruit, vegetables, forage, grains, timber, trees, bees and apiary
38 products, tobacco, other agricultural crops, general farming operation
39 purposes, native timber lands, or land that lays fallow. Agricultural use
40 may not be determined by the size of a parcel or size of a part of the
41 parcel. This subsection does not affect the assessment of any real
42 property assessed under IC 6-1.1-6 (assessment of certain forest lands),
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
50
1 IC 6-1.1-6.2 (assessment of certain windbreaks), or IC 6-1.1-6.7
2 (assessment of filter strips).
3 (c) The department of local government finance shall give written
4 notice to each county assessor of:
5 (1) the availability of the United States Department of
6 Agriculture's soil survey data; and
7 (2) the appropriate soil productivity factor for each type or
8 classification of soil shown on the United States Department of
9 Agriculture's soil survey map.
10 All assessing officials and the property tax assessment board of appeals
11 shall use the data in determining the true tax value of agricultural land.
12 However, notwithstanding the availability of new soil productivity
13 factors and the department of local government finance's notice of the
14 appropriate soil productivity factor for each type or classification of
15 soil shown on the United States Department of Agriculture's soil survey
16 map for the March 1, 2012, assessment date, the soil productivity
17 factors used for the March 1, 2011, assessment date shall be used for
18 the January 1, 2016, assessment date and each assessment date
19 thereafter.
20 (d) The department of local government finance shall by rule
21 provide for the method for determining the true tax value of each parcel
22 of agricultural land.
23 (e) This section does not apply to land purchased for industrial or
24 commercial uses.
25 (f) If an assessor changes the underlying parcel characteristics,
26 including age, grade, condition, property classification, or land
27 type previously assessed as agricultural land under this section, the
28 assessor bears the burden of proving that the change in underlying

1 section is a final unappealable order of the department of local

4 section the following apply:
5 (1) In the petition for review to the Indiana board, the county
6 assessor shall state what the county assessor contends the

29 established a reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions

39 for review may be appealed to the Indiana tax court as an

42 an interlocutory appeal brought under this subdivision.

42 thousand dollars ($500,000) until the fund reaches three million

1 dollars ($3,000,000).

27 board's determination does not create the presumption that the
28 county assessor's contentions are valid. A determination by the
29 Indiana board that the county assessor has established a
30 reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition
31 for review may be appealed to the Indiana tax court as an
32 interlocutory appeal. A party may petition for review by the
33 Indiana supreme court of the Indiana tax court's ruling regarding
34 an interlocutory appeal brought under this subdivision.
35 (4) The Indiana board shall not hold a hearing on the appeal under
36 IC 6-1.1-15-4 and the county assessor shall not be permitted to
37 conduct discovery under the Indiana board's administrative rules
38 until a determination has been issued under subdivision (3) and:
39 (A) any interlocutory appeal under subdivision (3) has been
40 ruled on by the Indiana tax court; or
41 (B) the Indiana supreme court has either rejected a petition for
42 review concerning the Indiana tax court's ruling on the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
59
1 interlocutory appeal or issued a decision regarding the Indiana
2 tax court's ruling on the interlocutory appeal.
3 (e) (c) On any appeal that has not been dismissed, the Indiana board
4 shall issue an order within one (1) year two (2) years after
5 (1) the taxpayer filed its petition for review.
6 (2) the issuance of the Indiana board's determination under
7 subsection (d)(3) in the case of an appeal by the county assessor;
8 or
9 (3) the Indiana tax court or the Indiana supreme court rules on a
10 taxpayer's interlocutory appeal under subsection (d)(3) in the case
11 of an appeal by the county assessor;
12 whichever is latest.
13 SECTION 45. IC 6-1.1-8.7-11 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
14 CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
15 [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 11. (a) As
16 used in this section, "fund" refers to the assessment of industrial
17 facilities fund established by subsection (b).
18 (b) The assessment of industrial facilities fund is established as
19 a dedicated fund to provide money to cover the costs incurred by
20 the department of local government finance during an industrial
21 facility assessment appeal.
22 (c) Money in the fund and income derived from the fund do not
23 revert to the state general fund at the end of a state fiscal year.
24 Money in the fund is continuously appropriated for the purposes
25 of the fund.
26 (d) The county fiscal officer shall transfer fifty thousand dollars
27 ($50,000) from the county's reassessment fund (as defined in
28 IC 6-1.1-4-27.5) before December 31 of each year to the state
29 comptroller for deposit in the fund. The county fiscal officer shall
30 stop payments to the fund once the balance of the fund reaches
31 three million dollars ($3,000,000). However, if the fund balance is
32 below two million dollars ($2,000,000) then the county fiscal officer
33 shall continue to deposit the annual fifty thousand dollars ($50,000)
34 until the fund reaches three million dollars ($3,000,000).
35 (e) The budget agency shall administer the fund.
36 (f) The budget committee shall review all expenditures from the
37 fund prior to the release of the funds.

23 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
24 JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]:
25 Chapter 10.2. Exemptions for Indiana Nonprofit Senior Living
26 Communities
27 Sec. 1. It is the intent of the general assembly that Indiana
28 nonprofit senior living communities identified in this chapter that
29 also meet the requirements set out in this chapter be exempt from
30 property taxation, including real and tangible property.
31 Sec. 2. All or part of a building is exempt from property
32 taxation if it is owned by an Indiana nonprofit entity that is:
33 (1) registered as a continuing care retirement community
34 under IC 23-2-4;
35 (2) defined as a small house health facility under
36 IC 16-18-2-331.9; or
37 (3) licensed as a health care or residential care facility under
38 IC 16-28.
39 Sec. 3. Tangible personal property is exempt from property
40 taxation if it is owned by an Indiana nonprofit entity that is:
41 (1) registered as a continuing care retirement community
42 under IC 23-2-4;
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
61
1 (2) defined as a small house health facility under
2 IC 16-18-2-331.9; or
3 (3) licensed as a health care or residential care facility under

20 chapter.
21 (b) An individual may claim a deduction from the assessed value of
22 the individual's homestead if:
23 (1) the individual served in the military or naval forces of the
24 United States for at least ninety (90) days;
25 (2) the individual received an honorable discharge;
26 (3) the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%);
27 (4) the individual's disability is evidenced by:
28 (A) a pension certificate or an award of compensation issued
29 by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs; or
30 (B) a certificate of eligibility issued to the individual by the
31 Indiana department of veterans' affairs after the Indiana
32 department of veterans' affairs has determined that the
33 individual's disability qualifies the individual to receive a
34 deduction under this section; and
35 (5) the homestead was conveyed without charge to the individual
36 who is the owner of the homestead by an organization that is
37 exempt from income taxation under the federal Internal Revenue
38 Code.
39 (c) If an individual is entitled to a deduction from assessed value
40 under subsection (b) for the individual's homestead, the amount of the
41 deduction is determined as follows:
42 (1) If the individual is totally disabled, the deduction is equal to
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
66
1 one hundred percent (100%) of the assessed value of the
2 homestead.
3 (2) If the individual has a disability of at least ninety percent
4 (90%) but the individual is not totally disabled, the deduction is
5 equal to ninety percent (90%) of the assessed value of the
6 homestead.
7 (3) If the individual has a disability of at least eighty percent
8 (80%) but less than ninety percent (90%), the deduction is equal
9 to eighty percent (80%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
10 (4) If the individual has a disability of at least seventy percent
11 (70%) but less than eighty percent (80%), the deduction is equal
12 to seventy percent (70%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
13 (5) If the individual has a disability of at least sixty percent (60%)
14 but less than seventy percent (70%), the deduction is equal to
15 sixty percent (60%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
16 (6) If the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%)
17 but less than sixty percent (60%), the deduction is equal to fifty
18 percent (50%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
19 (d) An individual who claims a deduction under this section for an
20 assessment date may not also claim a deduction under section 13
21 (before its expiration) or 14 of this chapter for that same assessment
22 date.
23 (e) An individual who desires to claim the deduction under this
24 section must claim the deduction in the manner specified by the
25 department of local government finance.

18 residence:
19 (A) that is located in Indiana;

21 (i) the individual owns;

32 216); or

36 chapter; (before its expiration); and
37 (C) that consists of a dwelling and includes up to one (1) acre
38 of land immediately surrounding that dwelling, and any of the
39 following improvements:
40 (i) Any number of decks, patios, gazebos, or pools.

9 the department of local government finance; and

11 subsection (c) and shall be held harmless by the department of
12 local government finance from any liability for the content of the
13 form.
14 (e) This subsection applies to a transaction that is closed after
15 December 31, 2009. In addition to providing the customer the form
16 described in subsection (c) before closing the transaction, a closing
17 agent shall do the following as soon as possible after the closing, and
18 within the time prescribed by the department of insurance under
19 IC 27-7-3-15.5:
20 (1) To the extent determinable, input the information described in
21 IC 27-7-3-15.5(c)(2) into the system maintained by the
22 department of insurance under IC 27-7-3-15.5.
23 (2) Submit the form described in IC 27-7-3-15.5(c) to the data
24 base described in IC 27-7-3-15.5(c)(2)(D).
25 (f) A closing agent to which this section applies shall document the
26 closing agent's compliance with this section with respect to each
27 transaction in the form of verification of compliance signed by the
28 customer.
29 (g) Subject to IC 27-7-3-15.5(d), a closing agent is subject to a civil
30 penalty of twenty-five dollars ($25) for each instance in which the
31 closing agent fails to comply with this section with respect to a
32 customer. The penalty:
33 (1) may be enforced by the state agency that has administrative
34 jurisdiction over the closing agent in the same manner that the
35 agency enforces the payment of fees or other penalties payable to
36 the agency; and
37 (2) shall be paid into:
38 (A) the state general fund, if the closing agent fails to comply
39 with subsection (b); or
40 (B) the home ownership education account established by
41 IC 5-20-1-27, if the closing agent fails to comply with
42 subsection (e) in a transaction that is closed after December
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
84
1 31, 2009.
2 (h) A closing agent is not liable for any other damages claimed by
3 a customer because of:
4 (1) the closing agent's mere failure to provide the appropriate
5 document to the customer under subsection (b); or
6 (2) with respect to a transaction that is closed after December 31,
7 2009, the closing agent's failure to input the information or
8 submit the form described in subsection (e).
9 (i) The state agency that has administrative jurisdiction over a
10 closing agent shall:
11 (1) examine the closing agent to determine compliance with this
12 section; and
13 (2) impose and collect penalties under subsection (g).

18 (B) the net assessed value of the fire protection district area
19 divided by one hundred (100).
20 The calculation under this subsection shall be used in the determination
21 of the fire protection district's maximum permissible ad valorem
22 property tax levy for property taxes first due and payable in the first
23 year of the increase and thereafter.

8 (A) the STEP ONE percentage; minus
9 (B) six percent (6%);
10 expressed as a decimal.

1 subsection (b) (after December 31, 2023), a school corporation is

7 under this subsection as determined under the following formula:
8 STEP ONE: Determine the amount of credits granted under this
9 chapter against the school corporation's levy for the school
10 corporation's operations fund.
11 STEP TWO: Determine the amount of the school corporation's
12 levy that is attributable to new debt incurred after June 30, 2019,

18 STEP FOUR: Subtract the STEP TWO amount from the STEP

20 STEP FIVE: Divide the STEP FOUR amount by the STEP
21 THREE amount expressed as a percentage.

23 percentage.

26 STEP EIGHT: Divide the STEP SIX amount by the STEP SEVEN

38 that year, determine whether the percentage computed by the school
39 corporation under subsection (c) is accurate and certify whether the
40 school corporation is eligible under this section.
41 (e) For a school corporation that is certified as eligible under this
42 section, the school corporation may allocate the effect of the credits

107
1 granted under this chapter and IC 6-1.1-49 proportionately among all
2 the school corporation's property tax funds that are not exempt under

4 and without taking into account the requirements of section 9.8 of this
5 chapter regarding protected taxes as determined under the following
6 formula:
7 STEP ONE: Determine the product of:
8 (A) the percentage determined under STEP EIGHT of
9 subsection (c); multiplied by
10 (B) five (5).
11 STEP TWO: Determine the lesser of the STEP ONE percentage
12 or one hundred percent (100%).
13 STEP THREE: Determine the product of:
14 (A) the amount determined under STEP SIX of subsection (c);
15 multiplied by
16 (B) the STEP TWO percentage.
17 The school corporation may allocate the amount of credits determined
18 under STEP THREE proportionately under this section. The
19 department of local government finance shall include in its certification
20 of an eligible school corporation under subsection (d) the amount of
21 credits that the school corporation may allocate proportionately as
22 determined under this subsection.
23 (f) This section expires January 1, 2027.

14 entitled to a credit against local property taxes imposed on the
15 individual's real property, or mobile home or manufactured home
16 within the county, if:
17 (1) the individual is at least sixty-five (65) years of age on or
18 before December 31 of the calendar year preceding the year in
19 which the credit is claimed;
20 (2) the individual has owned the real property, mobile home, or
21 manufactured home for at least one (1) year before claiming the
22 credit; or the individual has been buying the real property, mobile
23 home, or manufactured home under a contract that provides that
24 the individual is to pay the property taxes on the real property,
25 mobile home, or manufactured home for at least one (1) year
26 before claiming the credit, and the contract or a memorandum of
27 the contract is recorded in the county recorder's office;
28 (3) the individual:
29 (A) owns the real property, mobile home, or manufactured
30 home; or
31 (B) is buying the real property, mobile home, or manufactured
32 home under contract;
33 on the date the credit is claimed; and
34 (4) the:
35 (A) individual had, in the case of an individual who filed a
36 single return, adjusted gross income (as defined in Section 62
37 of the Internal Revenue Code) not exceeding sixty thousand
38 dollars ($60,000);
39 (B) individual had, in the case of an individual who filed a
40 joint income tax return with the individual's spouse, combined
41 adjusted gross income (as defined in Section 62 of the Internal
42 Revenue Code) not exceeding seventy thousand dollars
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
124
1 ($70,000); or
2 (C) combined adjusted gross income (as defined in Section 62
3 of the Internal Revenue Code) of the individual and all other
4 individuals with whom:
5 (i) the individual shares ownership; or
6 (ii) the individual is purchasing the property under a
7 contract;
8 as joint tenants or tenants in common did not exceed seventy
9 thousand dollars ($70,000);
10 for the calendar year preceding by two (2) years the calendar year
11 in which the property taxes are first due and payable; and
12 (5) the individual resides on the real property, mobile home,
13 or manufactured home.
14 (b) The amount of the credit is equal to one hundred fifty dollars
15 ($150).
16 (c) An individual may not be denied the credit provided under this
17 section because the individual is absent from the real property, mobile
18 home, or manufactured home while in a nursing home or hospital.
19 (d) For purposes of this section, if real property, a mobile home, or
20 a manufactured home is owned by:
21 (1) tenants by the entirety;
22 (2) joint tenants; or
23 (3) tenants in common;
24 only one (1) credit may be allowed. However, the age requirement is
25 satisfied if any one (1) of the tenants is at least sixty-five (65) years of
26 age.
27 (e) A surviving spouse is entitled to the credit provided by this
28 section if:
29 (1) the surviving spouse is at least sixty (60) years of age on or
30 before December 31 of the calendar year preceding the year in
31 which the credit is claimed;
32 (2) the surviving spouse's deceased husband or wife was at least
33 sixty-five (65) years of age at the time of a death; and
34 (3) the surviving spouse has not remarried.
35 (f) An individual who has sold real property to another person under
36 a contract that provides that the contract buyer is to pay the property
37 taxes on the real property may not claim the credit provided under this
38 section against that real property.
39 (g) If individuals share ownership or are purchasing the property
40 under a contract as joint tenants or tenants in common and all of the
41 tenants are not at least sixty-five (65) years of age, the credit allowed
42 under this section shall be reduced by an amount equal to the credit
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
125
1 multiplied by a fraction. The numerator of the fraction is the number of
2 tenants who are not at least sixty-five (65) years of age, and the
3 denominator is the total number of tenants.
4 (h) An individual wishing to claim a credit under this section must
5 file a statement, on forms prescribed by the department of local
6 government finance, with the county auditor and provide
7 documentation necessary to substantiate the individual's eligibility for
8 the credit. The statement must be completed and dated on or before
9 January 15 of the calendar year in which the property taxes are first due
10 and payable. The statement may be filed in person or by mail. If
11 mailed, the mailing must be postmarked on or before the last day for
12 filing. An individual who remains eligible for the credit in the
13 following year is not required to file a statement to apply for the credit
14 in the following year. However, an individual who receives a credit
15 under this section in a particular year and who becomes ineligible for
16 the credit in the following year shall notify the auditor of the county in
17 which the homestead is located of the individual's ineligibility not later
18 than sixty (60) days after the individual becomes ineligible.
19 SECTION 99. IC 6-1.1-51.3-5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA

32 issued by the United States Department of Veterans

36 department of veterans' affairs has determined that the

41 home; or

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

1 manufactured home under contract;
2 on the date the credit is claimed, and in the case of clause (B),

4 the county recorder's office.

6 dollars ($250).

8 provided by this section if:
9 (1) the individual satisfied the requirements of subsection
10 (a)(1) through (a)(4) at the time of death; or

12 (A) was killed in action;
13 (B) died while serving on active duty in the military or
14 naval forces of the United States; or
15 (C) died while performing inactive duty training in the
16 military or naval forces of the United States;
17 and the surviving spouse satisfies the requirement of subsection
18 (a)(5) at the time the credit is claimed. The surviving spouse is
19 entitled to the credit regardless of whether the property for which
20 the credit is claimed was owned by the deceased veteran or the
21 surviving spouse before the deceased veteran's death.
22 (d) An individual who receives the credit provided by this
23 section may receive any other property tax credit that the
24 individual is entitled to by law.
25 (e) An individual who has sold real property or a mobile home
26 or manufactured home to another person under a contract that
27 provides that the contract buyer is to pay the property taxes on the
28 real property, mobile home, or manufactured home may not claim
29 the credit provided under this section against that real property,
30 mobile home, or manufactured home.

33 local government finance, with the county auditor and provide
34 documentation necessary to substantiate the individual's eligibility
35 for the credit. The statement must be completed and dated on or
36 before January 15 of the calendar year in which the property taxes
37 are first due and payable. The statement may be filed in person or
38 by mail. If mailed, the mailing must be postmarked on or before
39 the last day for filing. An individual who remains eligible for the
40 credit in the following year is not required to file a statement to

42 who receives a credit under this section in a particular year and

4 SECTION 102. IC 6-2.5-5-29 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
5 FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 29. (a) As used in
6 this section:
7 "Manufactured home" means a manufactured home as that term is
8 defined in 42 U.S.C. 5402(6) as that statute was adopted and in effect
9 on January 1, 1988. has the definition set forth in IC 9-13-2-96(a).
10 The term includes a mobile home (as defined in IC 9-13-2-103.2).
11 "Industrialized residential structure" means a structure that is both
12 an industrialized building system (as defined in IC 22-12-1-14) and a
13 one (1) or two (2) family private residence.
14 (b) Sales of manufactured homes or industrialized residential
15 structures are exempt from the state gross retail tax to the extent that
16 the gross retail income from the sales is not attributable to the cost of
17 materials used in manufacturing the manufactured home or
18 industrialized residential structure.
19 (c) For purposes of this section, the part of the gross retail income
20 not attributable to the cost of materials used in manufacturing a
21 manufactured home or an industrialized residential structure is
22 thirty-five percent (35%) of the gross retail income derived from the
23 sale of the manufactured home or industrialized residential structure.
24 (d) The gross retail income derived from the sale of a preowned
25 manufactured home is exempt from the state gross retail tax.

21 amount of ten million dollars ($10,000,000);
22 (3) states an estimated capital investment of at least two
23 hundred fifty millions dollars ($250,000,000); and
24 (4) is for a project in a county having a population of more
25 than three hundred fifty thousand (350,000) and less than four
26 hundred thousand (400,000).
27 Notwithstanding subsection (a), for an agreement to which this
28 subsection applies, the corporation shall not enforce any
29 repayment provision relating to the credit awarded under this
30 chapter and shall amend the agreement to remove the repayment
31 provision not later than June 30, 2026.

26 this subsection for each individual who is a member of the fiscal body
27 of the county, city, or town as the voting record for that individual
28 either for or against the ordinance being proposed for consideration by
29 the local income tax council as a whole under section 9.5 of this
30 chapter. This subsection expires May 31, 2027. 2028.
31 (f) This subsection applies only to a county with a single voting bloc
32 that proposes to increase (but not decrease) a tax rate in the county. The
33 county auditor shall deliver copies of a proposed ordinance the auditor
34 receives under subsection (e) to the fiscal officers of all members of the
35 local income tax council (other than the member proposing the
36 ordinance under subsection (e)) within ten (10) days after receipt.
37 Subject to subsection (h), once a member receives a proposed
38 ordinance from the county auditor, the member shall vote on it within
39 thirty (30) days after receipt. This subsection expires May 31, 2027.
40 2028.
41 (g) This subsection applies only to a county with a single voting
42 bloc that proposes to increase (but not decrease) a tax rate in the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
143
1 county. The fiscal body of each county, city, or town voting on a
2 resolution to propose an ordinance under subsection (e), or voting on

5 on the resolution or the proposed ordinance. If an individual who sits

9 that individual's vote as a "no" vote against the resolution or the
10 proposed ordinance being considered, whichever is applicable, for

12 tally that the individual's "no" vote is due to absence or abstention. The
13 fiscal body of each county, city, or town shall certify the roll call vote
14 on a resolution or a proposed ordinance, either for or against, to the

12 and December 31 of a calendar year. If an adopting body adopts an

14 (1) IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(4);

17 that exceeds the applicable maximum tax rate or applicable
18 maximum aggregate tax rate allowable pursuant to IC 6-3.6-6-2,
19 IC 6-3.6-6-22, or IC 6-3.6-7, the department of local government
20 finance shall notify the adopting body and county fiscal officer or
21 municipal fiscal officer, as applicable, not later than thirty (30)
22 days after the adopting body submits the ordinance and
23 information required under IC 6-3.6-6-2 that one (1) or more tax
24 rates exceed the maximum allowable tax rate.

26 between January 1 and August 2 of a calendar year or October 2
27 and December 31 of a calendar year. Not later than thirty (30) days
28 after receiving a notification under subsection (b) from the
29 department of local government finance, the adopting body may
30 adopt an ordinance correcting the applicable tax rate or tax rates.
31 The following apply to an ordinance adopted under this subsection:
32 (1) Any statutory requirements for an ordinance that
33 otherwise apply to an ordinance adopted under this article to
34 impose a local income tax rate also apply to an ordinance
35 adopted under this subsection.
36 (2) If the tax rate or tax rates adopted in an ordinance
37 adopted under this subsection still exceed a maximum
38 allowable tax rate or maximum allowable aggregate tax rate,
39 the ordinance adopted under this subsection shall be
40 considered void and treated as if the adopting body did not
41 adopt any additional ordinance under this subsection.
42 (3) An ordinance adopted under this subsection has the same
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
145
1 effective date as the initial ordinance described in subsection
2 (b).
3 (d) If an adopting body adopts an ordinance between August 3
4 and October 1 of a calendar year to impose a local income tax that
5 exceeds a maximum allowable tax rate or rates, fails to adopt an
6 ordinance correcting the applicable tax rate or tax rates under
7 subsection (c), or, the ordinance is described in subsection (c)(2),
8 the tax rate or rates will be reduced according to the following:
9 (1) If a tax rate or tax rates imposed pursuant to
10 IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(4), IC 6-3.6-6-22, or
11 IC 6-3.6-7 exceed the maximum allowable rate specified in
12 IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(4), IC 6-3.6-6-22, or
13 IC 6-3.6-7, the tax rate or tax rates that exceed the maximum
14 allowable rate shall be reduced to the maximum allowable
15 rate without further action by the adopting body.
16 (2) If the aggregate tax rates imposed pursuant to
17 IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3) exceed the
18 maximum allowable aggregate rate in IC 6-3.6-6-2(c), the tax
19 rates shall be reduced without any further action by the
20 adopting body according to the following:
21 (A) Any portion of the aggregate tax rate that exceeds the
22 maximum allowable rate shall first be applied by reducing
23 the tax rate imposed under IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1), but may not
24 reduce the rate below the tax rate otherwise required
25 under this article.
26 (B) Any remaining portion of the aggregate tax rate that
27 exceeds the maximum allowable rate after the reduction in
28 clause (A) shall be applied to reduce the tax rates imposed
29 under IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(2) and IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3) in
30 proportion to the total rates imposed under
31 IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(2) and IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3).
32 (3) If the tax rate or rates exceed both the maximum allowable
33 rate specified in IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(1) through IC 6-3.6-6-2(b)(3)
34 and the maximum allowable aggregate tax rate in
35 IC 6-3.6-6-2(c), the tax rates shall first be reduced in the
36 manner set forth in subdivision (1) before application of the
37 reduction manner set forth in subdivision (2).
38 (4) Any tax rate reduction under this subsection has the same
39 effective date as the initial ordinance described in subsection
40 (b).

13 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2028]: Sec. 4.3. (a) Revenue raised from a tax
14 rate for fire protection and or emergency medical services under
15 section 2(b)(2) of this chapter shall be distributed by the county to
16 among the county and each fire protection district, fire protection
17 territory, and municipal fire department located within the county that
18 provides fire protection, emergency medical services, or both in the
19 county. Except as provided in subsection (b), at the discretion of the
20 county council, the county may distribute revenue raised from a tax rate
21 for fire protection and or emergency medical services under section
22 2(b)(2) of this chapter to township fire departments and volunteer fire
23 departments that provide fire protection, emergency medical
24 services, or both in the county.
25 (b) Revenue raised from a tax rate for fire protection and emergency
26 medical services under section 2(b)(2) of this chapter shall be allocated
27 to each fire protection district, fire protection territory, municipal fire
28 department, and, if applicable, township fire departments and volunteer
29 fire departments, based on the following formula:
30 STEP ONE: For each provider of fire protection and emergency
31 medical services located within the county that is eligible to
32 receive revenue under this section, determine the population
33 living within the service boundaries of the provider using the
34 most recent federal decennial census.
35 STEP TWO: For each provider of fire protection and emergency
36 medical services located within the county that is eligible to
37 receive revenue under this section, determine the number of
38 square miles within the service boundaries of the provider.
39 STEP THREE: For each provider of fire protection and
40 emergency medical services located within the county that is
41 eligible to receive revenue under this section, determine the
42 product of:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
152
1 (A) the STEP TWO amount; multiplied by
2 (B) twenty (20).
3 STEP FOUR: For each provider of fire protection and emergency
4 medical services located within the county that is eligible to
5 receive revenue under this section, determine the sum of:
6 (A) the STEP ONE result; plus
7 (B) the STEP THREE result.
8 STEP FIVE: Determine the sum total of the STEP FOUR results
9 for each provider of fire protection and emergency medical
10 services located within the county that is eligible to receive
11 revenue under this section.
12 STEP SIX: The percentage of revenue that shall be distributed to
13 each provider of fire protection and emergency medical services
14 located within the county that is eligible to receive revenue under
15 this section is equal to:
16 (A) the STEP FOUR result for the provider; divided by
17 (B) the STEP FIVE result.
18 (b) Subject to subsection (d), the county may determine the
19 allocation method for revenue raised from a tax rate for fire
20 protection or emergency medical services under section 2(b)(2) of
21 this chapter. However, in determining the allocation method, the
22 county shall, for each provider of fire protection, emergency
23 medical services, or both in the county, consider the service
24 boundaries of the provider and the population living within the
25 service boundaries of the provider using the most recent federal
26 decennial census.

30 being determined are carried out by full-time firefighters who

33 department under this section.

36 the entire county, only the part of the county in which the county
37 provides the fire protection, emergency medical services, or both
38 are considered within the service boundaries for the county.

40 distribution to a:
41 (1) fire protection territory shall be made to the provider unit
42 of the fire protection territory; and
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
153
1 (2) volunteer fire department shall be made to the taxing unit
2 that is served by the volunteer fire department.
3 (f) If the population living within the service boundaries of a
4 provider cannot be determined using data from the United States
5 Census Bureau, the county may determine an estimated population
6 based on income tax returns that report a residence located within
7 the service boundaries of the provider. The county auditor shall
