2 SECTION 133. IC 6-3.6-6-6.1, AS AMENDED BY THE
3 TECHNICAL CORRECTIONS BILL OF THE 2026 GENERAL
4 ASSEMBLY, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
5 JULY 1, 2028]: Sec. 6.1. (a) Revenue raised from a tax rate for certain
6 cities and towns under section 2(b)(4) of this chapter may shall be
7 distributed by the county to those cities and towns subject to the
8 provisions of this section and according to the following formula:
9 STEP ONE: Determine the population of each city and town
10 located in the county, excluding the population of any
11 municipality that:
12 (A) is eligible to impose a local income tax under section 22
13 of this chapter; and
14 (B) did not make an election under section 23(b)(3) of this
15 chapter.
16 STEP TWO: Determine the aggregate sum of the STEP ONE
17 results.
18 STEP THREE: Determine the sum of:
19 (A) the STEP TWO result; plus
20 (B) the population of the unincorporated area of the
21 county.
22 STEP FOUR: Divide the STEP TWO result by the STEP
23 THREE result.
24 STEP FIVE: Multiply the STEP FOUR result by one and
25 five-tenths (1.5), expressed as a percentage.
26 STEP SIX: Multiple the STEP FIVE result by the total
27 amount of revenue raised from the tax rate imposed under
28 section 2(b)(4) of this chapter.
29 STEP SEVEN: For each city and town located in the county
30 that adopted a resolution under subsection (d) for the year,
31 excluding any municipality that is eligible to impose a local
32 income tax under section 22 of this chapter and did not make
33 an election under section 23(b)(3) of this chapter, divide:
34 (A) the STEP ONE result for the city or town; by
35 (B) the STEP TWO result.
36 STEP EIGHT: To determine the amount to be allocated to
37 each city and town located in the county that adopted a
38 resolution under subsection (d) for the year, excluding any
39 municipality that is eligible to impose a local income tax
40 under section 22 of this chapter and did not make an election
41 under section 23(b)(3) of this chapter, multiply:
42 (A) the STEP SEVEN result for the city or town; by
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
156
1 (B) the STEP SIX result.
2 STEP NINE: Determine the aggregate sum of the STEP
3 EIGHT results for each city and town located in the county
4 that adopted a resolution under subsection (d) for the year,
5 excluding any municipality that is eligible to impose a local
6 income tax under section 22 of this chapter and did not make
7 an election under section 23(b)(3) of this chapter.
8 STEP TEN: Determine the result of:
9 (A) the total amount of revenue raised from the tax rate
10 imposed under section 2(b)(4) of this chapter; minus
11 (B) the STEP SIX result.
12 STEP ELEVEN: Determine the result of:
13 (A) the STEP SIX result; minus
14 (B) the STEP NINE result.
15 STEP TWELVE: To determine the amount to be allocated to
16 the county, determine the sum of:
17 (A) the STEP TEN result; plus
18 (B) the STEP ELEVEN result.

1 individual's personal automobile; or

1 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district

28 and STEP THREE amounts for the county.
29 If the boundaries of a taxing district change after the years for which a
30 ratio is calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE,
31 the state comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district
32 that reflects the tax rates imposed in the predecessor taxing districts. If
33 a new taxing district is established after the years for which a ratio is
34 calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE, the state
35 comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district and adjust
36 the ratio for other taxing districts in the county.
37 (d) Such determination shall be made from copies of vehicle
38 registration forms furnished by the bureau of motor vehicles. Prior to
39 such determination, the county assessor of each county shall, from
40 copies of registration forms, cause information pertaining to legal
41 residence of persons owning taxable vehicles to be verified from the
42 assessor's records, to the extent such verification can be so made. The
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
178
1 assessor shall further identify and verify from the assessor's records the
2 several taxing units within which such persons reside.
3 (e) Such verifications shall be done by not later than thirty (30) days
4 after receipt of vehicle registration forms by the county assessor, and
5 the assessor shall certify such information to the county auditor for the
6 auditor's use as soon as it is checked and completed.
7 (f) The taxing unit may deposit distributions received under this
8 chapter in any fund maintained by the taxing unit, and the
9 distributions may be used for any purpose allowed by law.

22 measuring the tax imposed by this chapter, the department shall

24 following classification plan:
25 CLASS DESCRIPTION
26 A Piston-driven
27 B Piston-driven,
28 and Pressurized
29 C Turbine driven
30 or other Powered
31 D Homebuilt, Gliders, or
32 Hot Air Balloons
33 (b) The tax imposed under this chapter is based on the age, class,
34 and maximum landing weight of the taxable aircraft. The amount of tax
35 imposed on the taxable aircraft is based on the following table:
36 Age Class A Class B Class C Class D
37 0-4 $.04/lb $.065/lb $.09/lb $.0175/lb
38 5-8 $.035/lb $.055/lb $.08/lb $.015/lb
39 9-12 $.03/lb $.05/lb $.07/lb $.0125/lb
40 13-16 $.025/lb $.025/lb $.025/lb $.01/lb
41 17-25 $.02/lb $.02/lb $.02/lb $.0075/lb
42 over 25 $.01/lb $.01/lb $.01/lb $.005/lb
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
180
1 (c) An aircraft owner, who sells an aircraft on which the owner has
2 paid the tax imposed under this chapter, is entitled to a credit for the
3 tax paid. The credit equals excise tax paid on the aircraft that was sold,
4 times the lesser of:
5 (1) ninety percent (90%); or
6 (2) ten percent (10%) times the number of months remaining in
7 the registration year after the sale of the aircraft.
8 The credit may only be used to reduce the tax imposed under this
9 chapter on another aircraft purchased by that owner during the
10 registration year in which the credit accrues. A person may not receive
11 a refund for a credit under this subsection.
12 (d) A person who is entitled to a property tax deduction under
13 IC 6-1.1-12-13 (before its expiration) or IC 6-1.1-12-14 is entitled to
14 a credit against the tax imposed on the person's aircraft under this
15 chapter. The credit equals the amount of the property tax deduction to
16 which the person is entitled under IC 6-1.1-12-13 (before its
17 expiration) and IC 6-1.1-12-14 minus the amount of that deduction
18 used to offset the person's property taxes or vehicle excise taxes, times
19 seven hundredths (.07). The credit may not exceed the amount of the
20 tax due under this chapter. The county auditor shall, upon the person's
21 request, furnish a certified statement showing the credit allowable
22 under this subsection. The department may not allow a credit under this
23 subsection until the auditor's statement has been filed in the
24 department's office.

12 IC 6-1.1-1-21, and "tuition support levy" refers to a school
13 corporation's tuition support property tax levy under IC 20-45-3-11
14 (repealed) for the school corporation's general fund. In order to
15 distribute the money in the county aircraft excise tax fund to the taxing
16 units of the county, the county auditor shall first allocate the money in
17 the fund among the taxing districts of the county. In making these
18 allocations, the county auditor shall allocate to a taxing district the
19 excise taxes collected with respect to aircraft usually located in the
20 taxing district when not in operation. Subject to this subsection, the
21 money allocated to a taxing district shall be apportioned and distributed
22 among the taxing units of that taxing district in the same manner and
23 at the same time that the property taxes are apportioned and distributed
24 (subject to adjustment as provided in IC 36-8-19-7.5). For purposes of
25 determining the distribution for a year under this section for a taxing
26 unit, a state welfare and tuition support allocation shall be deducted
27 from the total amount available for apportionment and distribution to
28 taxing units under this section before any apportionment and
29 distribution is made. The county auditor shall remit the state welfare
30 and tuition support allocation to the treasurer of state for deposit as
31 directed by the budget agency. The amount of the state welfare and
32 tuition support allocation for a county for a particular year is equal to
33 the result determined under STEP THREE of the following formula:
34 STEP ONE: Determine the result of the following:
35 (A) Separately for 2006, 2007, and 2008 for each taxing
36 district in the county, determine the result of:
37 (i) the tax rate imposed in the taxing district for the county's
38 county medical assistance to wards fund, family and
39 children's fund, children's psychiatric residential treatment
40 services fund, county hospital care for the indigent fund,
41 children with special health care needs county fund, plus, in
42 the case of Marion County, the tax rate imposed by the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
182
1 health and hospital corporation that was necessary to raise
2 thirty-five million dollars ($35,000,000) from all taxing
3 districts in the county; divided by
4 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
5 the same year.
6 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
7 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
8 (D) Determine the result of:
9 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
10 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
11 units in the taxing district; multiplied by
12 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
13 (E) Determine the sum of the clause (D) amounts for all taxing
14 districts in the county.
15 STEP TWO: Determine the result of the following:
16 (A) Separately for 2006, 2007, and 2008 for each taxing
17 district in the county, determine the result of:
18 (i) the tuition support levy tax rate imposed in the taxing
19 district plus the tax rate imposed by the school corporation
20 for the school corporation's special education preschool fund
21 in the district; divided by
22 (ii) the aggregate tax rate imposed in the taxing district for
23 the same year.
24 (B) Determine the sum of the clause (A) amounts.
25 (C) Divide the clause (B) amount by three (3).
26 (D) Determine the result of:
27 (i) the amount of excise taxes allocated to the taxing district
28 that would otherwise be available for distribution to taxing
29 units in the taxing district; multiplied by
30 (ii) the clause (C) amount.
31 (E) Determine the sum of the clause (D) amounts for all taxing
32 districts in the county.
33 STEP THREE: Determine the sum of the STEP ONE and STEP
34 TWO amounts for the county.
35 If the boundaries of a taxing district change after the years for which a
36 ratio is calculated under STEP ONE or STEP TWO, the state
37 comptroller shall establish a ratio for the new taxing district that
38 reflects the tax rates imposed in the predecessor taxing districts. If a
39 new taxing district is established after the years for which a ratio is
40 calculated under STEP ONE, STEP TWO, or STEP THREE, the state

12 (15,000) and less than two hundred thousand (200,000) based on
13 the population of the most recent decennial census in the city.
14 (b) The fiscal officer of each city under subsection (a) shall
15 establish a municipal tourism capital fund. The fiscal officer shall
16 deposit in the fund all money received by the city under this
17 section. The city fiscal body shall administer the fund. The city may
18 not establish a tourism board or similar entity for any purposes of
19 the fund and the city fiscal body shall have sole authority regarding
20 the use of money in the fund as set forth under subsection (c).
21 (c) Money in the fund may be used only for capital projects for
22 tourism related purposes as determined by the city fiscal body. The
23 city fiscal body may issue bonds, enter into leases, or incur other
24 obligations for the purposes of this subsection.
25 (d) Money transferred to a city under subsection (a) shall not be
26 used by the city for tourism marketing, tourism promotion, or
27 tourism planning purposes.

4 ASSEMBLY, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
5 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 3. (a) The fiscal body of a county may levy a tax
6 on every person engaged in the business of renting or furnishing, for
7 periods of less than thirty (30) days, any room or rooms, lodgings, or
8 accommodations in any:
9 (1) hotel;
10 (2) motel;
11 (3) boat motel;
12 (4) inn;
13 (5) college or university memorial union;
14 (6) college or university residence hall or dormitory; or
15 (7) tourist cabin;
16 located in the county.
17 (b) The tax does not apply to gross income received in a transaction
18 in which:
19 (1) a student rents lodgings in a college or university residence
20 hall while that student participates in a course of study for which
21 the student receives college credit from a college or university
22 located in the county; or
23 (2) a person rents a room, lodging, or accommodations for a
24 period of thirty (30) days or more.
25 (c) The tax may not exceed:
26 (1) the rate of five percent (5%) in a county other than a county
27 subject to subdivision (2), (3), or (4), or (5);
28 (2) after June 30, 2019, and except as provided in section 6.7 of
29 this chapter, the rate of eight percent (8%) in Howard County; or
30 (3) after June 30, 2021, the rate of nine percent (9%) in Daviess
31 County;
32 (4) subject to subsection (g), after June 30, 2026, the rate of
33 eight percent (8%) in DeKalb County; or
34 (5) subject to subsection (g), after June 30, 2026, the rate of
35 eight percent (8%) in Noble County.
36 The tax is imposed on the gross retail income derived from lodging
37 income only and is in addition to the state gross retail tax imposed
38 under IC 6-2.5.
39 (d) The county fiscal body may adopt an ordinance to require that
40 the tax shall be paid monthly to the county treasurer. If such an
41 ordinance is adopted, the tax shall be paid to the county treasurer not
42 more than twenty (20) days after the end of the month the tax is
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
201
1 collected. If such an ordinance is not adopted, the tax shall be imposed,
2 paid, and collected in exactly the same manner as the state gross retail
3 tax is imposed, paid, and collected under IC 6-2.5.

5 liabilities, procedures, penalties, definitions, exemptions, and
6 administration are applicable to the imposition and administration of
7 the tax imposed under this section except to the extent those provisions
8 are in conflict or inconsistent with the specific provisions of this
9 chapter or the requirements of the county treasurer. If the tax is paid to

23 (5%) shall expire January 1, 2049.

15 (c) All terms of office of commission members begin on January 1.
16 Initial appointments must be for staggered terms, with subsequent
17 appointments for two (2) year terms. A member whose term expires
18 may be reappointed to serve another term. If a vacancy occurs, the
19 appointing authority shall appoint a qualified person to serve for the
20 remainder of the term. If an initial appointment is not made by
21 February 1 or a vacancy is not filled within thirty (30) days, the
22 commission shall appoint a member by majority vote.
23 (d) A member of the commission may be removed for cause by the
24 member's appointing authority.
25 (e) Members of the commission may not receive a salary. However,
26 commission members are entitled to reimbursement for necessary
27 expenses incurred in the performance of their respective duties.
28 (f) Each commission member, before entering the member's duties,
29 shall take an oath of office in the usual form, to be endorsed upon the
30 member's certificate of appointment and promptly filed with the clerk
31 of the circuit court of the county.
32 (g) The commission shall meet after January 1 each year for the
33 purpose of organization. It shall elect one (1) of its members president,
34 another vice president, another secretary, and another treasurer. The
35 members elected to those offices shall perform the duties pertaining to
36 the offices. The first officers chosen shall serve from the date of their
37 election until their successors are elected and qualified. A majority of
38 the commission constitutes a quorum, and the concurrence of a
39 majority of the commission is necessary to authorize any action.
40 (h) The commission must also include a member appointed by
41 the city executive of each city within the county.
42 SECTION 188. IC 6-9-56-7 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY 1,

1 2026]. Sec. 7. (a) The county treasurer shall deposit in the convention,

15 CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
16 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 7.5. (a) For purposes of this
17 section, "fund" refers to the convention, visitor, tourism
18 promotion, and capital fund established under section 4(a)(2) of
19 this chapter.
20 (b) The county treasurer shall deposit in the fund the amount of
21 money received under section 3 of this chapter that is not more
22 than five percent (5%).
23 (c) Money in the fund shall be expended only as provided in
24 section 4(c) of this chapter.
25 (d) The commission may transfer money in the fund to any
26 Indiana nonprofit corporation for the purpose of promotion and
27 encouragement in the county of conventions, trade shows, visitors,
28 or special events. The commission may transfer money under this
29 subsection only after approving the transfer. The commission may
30 transfer money under this subsection on a monthly basis or at
31 another frequency as determined by the commission.
32 (e) The commission must approve any transfer of money from
33 the fund and may transfer money from the fund to support capital
34 projects in the county that promote long term tourism, convention,
35 or recreation projects proposed by any of the following:
36 (1) The county government.
37 (2) A separate body corporate and politic in Hamilton County.
38 (3) Any Indiana nonprofit corporation in Hamilton County.
39 The commission may transfer money under this subsection on a
40 monthly basis or at another frequency as determined by the
41 commission.
42 (f) The commission may also review and approve proposals
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
215
1 submitted by applicants that seek money from the fund with the
2 purpose and view of enhancing or providing support for capital
3 projects that promote long term tourism, convention, or other
4 economic development related to recreation. Funding available
5 under this subsection shall be made available on an annual basis.
6 In determining whether to provide funding to a particular capital
7 project under this subsection, the commission may use the
8 following factors as a guide for capital project funding:
9 (1) The proposed capital project is believed to be economically
10 sound to the Hamilton County tourism, convention, or
11 recreation economy and is also believed to be beneficial to:
12 (A) the general population of Hamilton County; or
13 (B) a particular location in Hamilton County.
14 (2) The proposed capital project provides for reasonably
15 adequate public assembly, gathering, or entertainment space
16 and is integrally related to enhancing the tourism, convention,
17 or recreation opportunities in Hamilton County or a
18 particular location in Hamilton County.
19 (3) The commission makes a reasonable effort to assess
20 whether a proposed capital project aligns with the purpose of
21 the commission and has a direct, indirect, or supportive
22 relationship to the mission and promotional efforts of the
23 commission as established and funded by the fund.
24 Any remaining funds collected that are not awarded during an
25 application period revert to the fund and may be used for
26 distribution in a subsequent application period.
27 (g) An applicant that receives a grant of money from the fund
28 under subsection (f):
29 (1) must agree to provide to the commission proof of project
30 completion, including proof that the project was completed
31 through the use of the grant money; and
32 (2) may be subject to annual financial reporting and audit.

2 SECTION 191. IC 6-9-56-8.5 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA

4 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8.5. (a) The county treasurer shall
5 transfer the amount of money received under section 3(c)(2) of this
6 chapter that is generated by a rate that exceeds five percent (5%)
7 to the fiscal officer of each of the following cities with each city
8 receiving an equal twenty-five percent (25%) share of the total
9 amount collected:
10 (1) Noblesville.
11 (2) Carmel.
12 (3) Fishers.
13 (4) Westfield.

15 establish a municipal tourism capital fund. The fiscal officer shall
16 deposit in the fund all money received by the city under this
17 section. The city fiscal body shall administer the fund. The city may
18 not establish a tourism board or similar entity for any purposes of
19 the fund and the city fiscal body shall have sole authority regarding
20 the use of money in the fund as set forth under subsection (c).
21 (c) Money in the fund may be used only for capital projects for
22 tourism related purposes as determined by the city fiscal body. The
23 city fiscal body may issue bonds, enter into leases, or incur other
24 obligations for the purposes of this subsection.
25 (d) Money transferred to a city under subsection (a) shall not be
26 used by the city for tourism marketing, tourism promotion, or
27 tourism planning purposes.

32 convention, visitor, and tourism industry in the county. If two (2) or
33 more adjoining counties desire to establish a joint commission, the
34 counties shall enter into an agreement under IC 36-1-7.

37 commission must also include any additional members appointed
38 under subsection (h). Each of the members must be:
39 (1) engaged in a convention, visitor, or tourism business; or
40 (2) involved in or promoting conventions, visitors, or tourism.

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
218
1 not required to reside in the county but must reside in Indiana. A
2 member who is not an owner or an executive level employee of a
3 convention, visitor, or tourism related business located in the county
4 must reside in the county. If available and willing to serve, at least two
5 (2) of the members must be engaged in the business of renting or
6 furnishing rooms, lodging, or accommodations (as described in section
7 3 of this chapter). The county executive shall also determine who will
8 make the appointments to the commission.
9 (c) All terms of office of commission members begin on January 1.

15 February 1 or a vacancy is not filled within thirty (30) days, the
16 commission shall appoint a member by majority vote.

25 SECTION 196. IC 6-9-78.1 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
26 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
27 JULY 1, 2026]:
28 Chapter 78.1. Lagro Food and Beverage Tax
29 Sec. 1. This chapter applies to the town of Lagro.
30 Sec. 2. The definitions in IC 6-9-12-1 apply throughout this
31 chapter.
32 Sec. 3. (a) The fiscal body of the town may adopt an ordinance
33 to impose an excise tax, known as the town food and beverage tax,
34 on transactions described in section 4 of this chapter. The fiscal
35 body of the town may adopt an ordinance under this subsection
36 only after the town fiscal body has previously:
37 (1) adopted a resolution in support of the proposed town food
38 and beverage tax; and
39 (2) held at least one (1) separate public hearing in which a
40 discussion of the proposed ordinance to impose the town food
41 and beverage tax is the only substantive issue on the agenda
42 for the public hearing.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
223
1 (b) If the town fiscal body adopts an ordinance under subsection
2 (a), the town fiscal body shall immediately send a certified copy of
3 the ordinance to the department of state revenue.
4 (c) If the town fiscal body adopts an ordinance under subsection
5 (a), the town food and beverage tax applies to transactions that
6 occur after the last day of the month following the month in which
7 the ordinance is adopted.
8 Sec. 4. (a) Except as provided in subsection (c), a tax imposed
9 under section 3 of this chapter applies to a transaction in which
10 food or beverage is furnished, prepared, or served:
11 (1) for consumption at a location or on equipment provided by
12 a retail merchant;
13 (2) in the town; and
14 (3) by a retail merchant for consideration.
15 (b) Transactions described in subsection (a)(1) include
16 transactions in which food or beverage is:
17 (1) served by a retail merchant off the merchant's premises;
18 (2) food sold in a heated state or heated by a retail merchant;
19 (3) made of two (2) or more food ingredients, mixed or
20 combined by a retail merchant for sale as a single item (other
21 than food that is only cut, repackaged, or pasteurized by the
22 seller, and eggs, fish, meat, poultry, and foods containing these
23 raw animal foods requiring cooking by the consumer as
24 recommended by the federal Food and Drug Administration
25 in chapter 3, subpart 3-401.11 of its Food Code so as to
26 prevent food borne illnesses); or
27 (4) food sold with eating utensils provided by a retail
28 merchant, including plates, knives, forks, spoons, glasses,
29 cups, napkins, or straws (for purposes of this subdivision, a
30 plate does not include a container or package used to
31 transport the food).
32 (c) The town food and beverage tax does not apply to the
33 furnishing, preparing, or serving of a food or beverage in a
34 transaction that is exempt, or to the extent the transaction is
35 exempt, from the state gross retail tax imposed by IC 6-2.5.
36 Sec. 5. The town food and beverage tax rate:
37 (1) must be imposed in an increment of twenty-five
38 hundredths percent (0.25%); and
39 (2) may not exceed one percent (1%);
40 of the gross retail income received by the merchant from the food
41 or beverage transaction described in section 4 of this chapter. For
42 purposes of this chapter, the gross retail income received by the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
224
1 retail merchant from a transaction does not include the amount of
2 tax imposed on the transaction under IC 6-2.5.
3 Sec. 6. A tax imposed under this chapter shall be imposed, paid,
4 and collected in the same manner that the state gross retail tax is
5 imposed, paid, and collected under IC 6-2.5. However, the return
6 to be filed with the payment of the tax imposed under this chapter
7 may be made on a separate return or may be combined with the
8 return filed for the payment of the state gross retail tax, as
9 prescribed by the department of state revenue.
10 Sec. 7. The amounts received from the tax imposed under this
11 chapter shall be paid monthly by the treasurer of state to the town
12 fiscal officer upon warrants issued by the state comptroller.
13 Sec. 8. (a) If a tax is imposed under section 3 of this chapter by
14 the town, the town fiscal officer shall establish a food and beverage
15 tax receipts fund.
16 (b) The town fiscal officer shall deposit in the fund all amounts
17 received under this chapter.
18 (c) Money earned from the investment of money in the fund
19 becomes a part of the fund.
20 Sec. 9. Money in the food and beverage tax receipts fund must
21 be used by the town only for the following purposes:
22 (1) For economic development purposes, including the pledge
23 of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases, or other
24 obligations for economic development purposes.
25 (2) For park and recreation purposes, including the purchase
26 of land for park and recreation purposes.
27 (3) The pledge of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases,
28 or other obligations incurred for a purpose described in
29 subdivision (2).
30 Sec. 10. With respect to obligations for which a pledge has been
31 made under section 9 of this chapter, the general assembly
32 covenants with the holders of the obligations that this chapter will
33 not be repealed or amended in a manner that will adversely affect
34 the imposition or collection of the tax imposed under this chapter
35 if the payment of any of the obligations is outstanding.
36 Sec. 11. (a) If the town imposes the tax authorized by this
37 chapter, the tax terminates on January 1, 2049.
38 (b) This chapter expires January 1, 2049.
39 SECTION 197. IC 6-9-78.2 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
40 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
41 UPON PASSAGE]:
42 Chapter 78.2. Rush County Food and Beverage Tax
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
225
1 Sec. 1. This chapter applies to Rush County.
2 Sec. 2. The definitions in IC 6-9-12-1 apply throughout this
3 chapter.
4 Sec. 3. (a) The fiscal body of the county may adopt an ordinance
5 on or before December 31, 2026, to impose an excise tax, known as
6 the county food and beverage tax, on transactions described in
7 section 4 of this chapter. The fiscal body of the county may adopt
8 an ordinance under this subsection only after the county fiscal
9 body has previously held at least one (1) separate public hearing in
10 which a discussion of the proposed ordinance to impose the county
11 food and beverage tax is the only substantive issue on the agenda
12 for the public hearing.
13 (b) If the county fiscal body adopts an ordinance under
14 subsection (a), the county fiscal body shall immediately send a
15 certified copy of the ordinance to the department of state revenue.
16 (c) If the county fiscal body adopts an ordinance under
17 subsection (a), the county food and beverage tax applies to
18 transactions that occur after the later of the following:
19 (1) The day specified in the ordinance.
20 (2) The last day of the month that succeeds the month in
21 which the ordinance is adopted.
22 Sec. 4. (a) Except as provided in subsection (c), a tax imposed
23 under section 3 of this chapter applies to a transaction in which
24 food or beverage is furnished, prepared, or served:
25 (1) for consumption at a location or on equipment provided by
26 a retail merchant;
27 (2) in the county in which the tax is imposed; and
28 (3) by a retail merchant for consideration.
29 (b) Transactions described in subsection (a)(1) include
30 transactions in which food or beverage is:
31 (1) served by a retail merchant off the merchant's premises;
32 (2) sold in a heated state or heated by a retail merchant;
33 (3) made of two (2) or more food ingredients, mixed or
34 combined by a retail merchant for sale as a single item (other
35 than food that is only cut, repackaged, or pasteurized by the
36 seller, and eggs, fish, meat, poultry, and foods containing these
37 raw animal foods requiring cooking by the consumer as
38 recommended by the federal Food and Drug Administration
39 in chapter 3, subpart 3-401.11 of its Food Code so as to
40 prevent food borne illnesses); or
41 (4) sold with eating utensils provided by a retail merchant,
42 including plates, knives, forks, spoons, glasses, cups, napkins,
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
226
1 or straws (for purposes of this subdivision, a plate does not
2 include a container or package used to transport food).
3 (c) The county food and beverage tax does not apply to the
4 furnishing, preparing, or serving of a food or beverage in a
5 transaction that is exempt, or to the extent the transaction is
6 exempt, from the state gross retail tax imposed by IC 6-2.5.
7 Sec. 5. The county food and beverage tax rate:
8 (1) must be imposed in an increment of twenty-five
9 hundredths percent (0.25%); and
10 (2) may not exceed one percent (1%);
11 of the gross retail income received by the merchant from the food
12 or beverage transaction described in section 4 of this chapter. For
13 purposes of this chapter, the gross retail income received by the
14 retail merchant from a transaction does not include the amount of
15 tax imposed on the transaction under IC 6-2.5.
16 Sec. 6. A tax imposed under this chapter is imposed, paid, and
17 collected in the same manner that the state gross retail tax is
18 imposed, paid, and collected under IC 6-2.5. However, the return
19 to be filed with the payment of the tax imposed under this chapter
20 may be made on a separate return or may be combined with the
21 return filed for the payment of the state gross retail tax, as
22 prescribed by the department of state revenue.
23 Sec. 7. The amounts received from the tax imposed under this
24 chapter shall be paid monthly by the treasurer of state to the
25 county fiscal officer upon warrants issued by the state comptroller.
26 Sec. 8. (a) If a tax is imposed under section 3 of this chapter by
27 the county, the county fiscal officer shall establish a food and
28 beverage tax receipts fund.
29 (b) The county fiscal officer shall deposit in the fund all amounts
30 received under this chapter.
31 (c) Money earned from the investment of money in the fund
32 becomes a part of the fund.
33 Sec. 9. Money in the food and beverage tax receipts fund must
34 be used by the county only for the following purposes:
35 (1) Economic development and tourism related purposes or
36 facilities, including the purchase of land for economic
37 development or tourism related purposes.
38 (2) The pledge of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases,
39 or other obligations incurred for a purpose described in
40 subdivision (1).
41 Revenue derived from the imposition of a tax under this chapter
42 may be treated by the county as additional revenue for the purpose
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
227
1 of fixing its budget for the budget year during which the revenues
2 are to be distributed to the county.
3 Sec. 10. With respect to obligations for which a pledge has been
4 made under section 9 of this chapter, the general assembly
5 covenants with the holders of the obligations that this chapter will
6 not be repealed or amended in a manner that will adversely affect
7 the imposition or collection of the tax imposed under this chapter
8 if the payment of any of the obligations is outstanding.
9 Sec. 11. (a) If the county imposes the tax authorized by this
10 chapter, the tax terminates on January 1, 2049.
11 (b) This chapter expires January 1, 2049.
12 SECTION 198. IC 6-9-78.3 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
13 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
14 JULY 1, 2026]:
15 Chapter 78.3. Greendale Food and Beverage Tax
16 Sec. 1. This chapter applies to the city of Greendale.
17 Sec. 2. The definitions in IC 6-9-12-1 apply throughout this
18 chapter.
19 Sec. 3. (a) The fiscal body of the city may adopt an ordinance to
20 impose an excise tax, known as the city food and beverage tax, on
21 transactions described in section 4 of this chapter. The fiscal body
22 of the city may adopt an ordinance under this subsection only after
23 the city fiscal body has previously:
24 (1) adopted a resolution in support of the proposed city food
25 and beverage tax; and
26 (2) held at least one (1) separate public hearing in which a
27 discussion of the proposed ordinance to impose the city food
28 and beverage tax is the only substantive issue on the agenda
29 for the public hearing.
30 (b) If the city fiscal body adopts an ordinance under subsection
31 (a), the city fiscal body shall immediately send a certified copy of
32 the ordinance to the department of state revenue.
33 (c) If the city fiscal body adopts an ordinance under subsection
34 (a), the city food and beverage tax applies to transactions that
35 occur after the last day of the month following the month in which
36 the ordinance is adopted.
37 Sec. 4. (a) Except as provided in subsection (c), a tax imposed
38 under section 3 of this chapter applies to a transaction in which
39 food or beverage is furnished, prepared, or served:
40 (1) for consumption at a location or on equipment provided by
41 a retail merchant;
42 (2) in the city; and
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
228
1 (3) by a retail merchant for consideration.
2 (b) Transactions described in subsection (a)(1) include
3 transactions in which food or beverage is:
4 (1) served by a retail merchant off the merchant's premises;
5 (2) sold in a heated state or heated by a retail merchant;
6 (3) made of two (2) or more food ingredients, mixed or
7 combined by a retail merchant for sale as a single item (other
8 than food that is only cut, repackaged, or pasteurized by the
9 seller, and eggs, fish, meat, poultry, and foods containing these
10 raw animal foods requiring cooking by the consumer as
11 recommended by the federal Food and Drug Administration
12 in chapter 3, subpart 3-401.11 of its Food Code so as to
13 prevent food borne illnesses); or
14 (4) sold with eating utensils provided by a retail merchant,
15 including plates, knives, forks, spoons, glasses, cups, napkins,
16 or straws (for purposes of this subdivision, a plate does not
17 include a container or package used to transport the food).
18 (c) The city food and beverage tax does not apply to the
19 furnishing, preparing, or serving of a food or beverage in a
20 transaction that is exempt, or to the extent the transaction is
21 exempt, from the state gross retail tax imposed by IC 6-2.5.
22 Sec. 5. The city food and beverage tax rate:
23 (1) must be imposed in an increment of twenty-five
24 hundredths percent (0.25%); and
25 (2) may not exceed one percent (1%);
26 of the gross retail income received by the merchant from the food
27 or beverage transaction described in section 4 of this chapter. For
28 purposes of this chapter, the gross retail income received by the
29 retail merchant from a transaction does not include the amount of
30 tax imposed on the transaction under IC 6-2.5.
31 Sec. 6. A tax imposed under this chapter shall be imposed, paid,
32 and collected in the same manner that the state gross retail tax is
33 imposed, paid, and collected under IC 6-2.5. However, the return
34 to be filed with the payment of the tax imposed under this chapter
35 may be made on a separate return or may be combined with the
36 return filed for the payment of the state gross retail tax, as
37 prescribed by the department of state revenue.
38 Sec. 7. The amounts received from the tax imposed under this
39 chapter shall be paid monthly by the treasurer of state to the city
40 fiscal officer upon warrants issued by the state comptroller.
41 Sec. 8. (a) If a tax is imposed under section 3 of this chapter by
42 the city, the city fiscal officer shall establish a food and beverage
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
229
1 tax receipts fund.
2 (b) The city fiscal officer shall deposit in the fund all amounts
3 received under this chapter.
4 (c) Money earned from the investment of money in the fund
5 becomes a part of the fund.
6 Sec. 9. Money in the food and beverage tax receipts fund must
7 be used by the city only for the following purposes:
8 (1) Park and recreation purposes, including the purchase of
9 land for park and recreation purposes.
10 (2) Economic development and tourism related purposes or
11 facilities, including the purchase of land for economic
12 development or tourism related purposes.
13 (3) The pledge of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases,
14 or other obligations incurred for a purpose described in
15 subdivisions (1) and (2).
16 Sec. 10. With respect to obligations for which a pledge has been
17 made under section 9 of this chapter, the general assembly
18 covenants with the holders of the obligations that this chapter will
19 not be repealed or amended in a manner that will adversely affect
20 the imposition or collection of the tax imposed under this chapter
21 if the payment of any of the obligations is outstanding.
22 Sec. 11. (a) If the city imposes the tax authorized by this chapter,
23 the tax terminates on January 1, 2049.
24 (b) This chapter expires January 1, 2049.
25 SECTION 199. IC 6-9-78.4 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
26 AS A NEW CHAPTER TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
27 JULY 1, 2026]:
28 Chapter 78.4. Huntington Food and Beverage Tax
29 Sec. 1. This chapter applies to one (1) but not both:
30 (1) Huntington County; or
31 (2) the city of Huntington.
32 If Huntington County is the first to adopt an ordinance under
33 section 3 of this chapter to impose a food and beverage tax, the city
34 of Huntington is thereafter prohibited from imposing a food and
35 beverage tax under this chapter. If the city of Huntington is the
36 first to adopt an ordinance under section 3 of this chapter to
37 impose a food and beverage tax, Huntington County is thereafter
38 prohibited from imposing a food and beverage tax under this
39 chapter.
40 Sec. 2. (a) The definitions in IC 6-9-12-1 apply throughout this
41 chapter.
42 (b) For purposes of this chapter, "adopting body" means either
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
230
1 Huntington County or the city of Huntington, whichever is first to
2 adopt a food and beverage tax under this chapter.
3 Sec. 3. (a) The fiscal body of the adopting body may adopt an
4 ordinance to impose an excise tax, known as a food and beverage
5 tax, on transactions described in section 4 of this chapter. The
6 fiscal body of the adopting body may adopt an ordinance under
7 this subsection only after the fiscal body of the adopting body has
8 previously:
9 (1) adopted a resolution in support of the proposed food and
10 beverage tax; and
11 (2) held at least one (1) separate public hearing in which a
12 discussion of the proposed ordinance to impose the food and
13 beverage tax is the only substantive issue on the agenda for
14 the public hearing.
15 (b) If the fiscal body of the adopting body adopts an ordinance
16 under subsection (a), the fiscal body shall immediately send a
17 certified copy of the ordinance to the department of state revenue.
18 (c) If the fiscal body of the adopting body adopts an ordinance
19 under subsection (a), the food and beverage tax applies to
20 transactions that occur after the last day of the month following
21 the month in which the ordinance is adopted.
22 Sec. 4. (a) Except as provided in subsection (c), a tax imposed
23 under section 3 of this chapter applies to a transaction in which
24 food or beverage is furnished, prepared, or served:
25 (1) for consumption at a location or on equipment provided by
26 a retail merchant;
27 (2) in the boundary of the adopting body; and
28 (3) by a retail merchant for consideration.
29 (b) Transactions described in subsection (a)(1) include
30 transactions in which food or beverage is:
31 (1) served by a retail merchant off the merchant's premises;
32 (2) food sold in a heated state or heated by a retail merchant;
33 (3) made of two (2) or more food ingredients, mixed or
34 combined by a retail merchant for sale as a single item (other
35 than food that is only cut, repackaged, or pasteurized by the
36 seller, and eggs, fish, meat, poultry, and foods containing these
37 raw animal foods requiring cooking by the consumer as
38 recommended by the federal Food and Drug Administration
39 in chapter 3, subpart 3-401.11 of its Food Code so as to
40 prevent food borne illnesses); or
41 (4) food sold with eating utensils provided by a retail
42 merchant, including plates, knives, forks, spoons, glasses,
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
231
1 cups, napkins, or straws (for purposes of this subdivision, a
2 plate does not include a container or package used to
3 transport the food).
4 (c) The food and beverage tax does not apply to the furnishing,
5 preparing, or serving of a food or beverage in a transaction that is
6 exempt, or to the extent the transaction is exempt, from the state
7 gross retail tax imposed by IC 6-2.5.
8 Sec. 5. The food and beverage tax rate:
9 (1) must be imposed in an increment of twenty-five
10 hundredths percent (0.25%); and
11 (2) may not exceed one percent (1%);
12 of the gross retail income received by the merchant from the food
13 or beverage transaction described in section 4 of this chapter. For
14 purposes of this chapter, the gross retail income received by the
15 retail merchant from a transaction does not include the amount of
16 tax imposed on the transaction under IC 6-2.5.
17 Sec. 6. A tax imposed under this chapter shall be imposed, paid,
18 and collected in the same manner that the state gross retail tax is
19 imposed, paid, and collected under IC 6-2.5. However, the return
20 to be filed with the payment of the tax imposed under this chapter
21 may be made on a separate return or may be combined with the
22 return filed for the payment of the state gross retail tax, as
23 prescribed by the department of state revenue.
24 Sec. 7. The amounts received from the tax imposed under this
25 chapter shall be paid monthly by the treasurer of state to the fiscal
26 body of the adopting body upon warrants issued by the state
27 comptroller.
28 Sec. 8. (a) If a tax is imposed under section 3 of this chapter by
29 the adopting body, the fiscal body of the adopting body shall
30 establish a food and beverage tax receipts fund.
31 (b) The fiscal body of the adopting body shall deposit in the fund
32 all amounts received under this chapter.
33 (c) Money earned from the investment of money in the fund
34 becomes a part of the fund.
35 Sec. 9. Money in the food and beverage tax receipts fund must
36 be used by the adopting body only for the following purposes:
37 (1) For economic development purposes, including the pledge
38 of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases, or other
39 obligations for economic development purposes.
40 (2) For park and recreation purposes, including the purchase
41 of land for park and recreation purposes.
42 (3) The pledge of money under IC 5-1-14-4 for bonds, leases,
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
232
1 or other obligations incurred for a purpose described in
2 subdivision (2).
3 Sec. 10. With respect to obligations for which a pledge has been
4 made under section 9 of this chapter, the general assembly
5 covenants with the holders of the obligations that this chapter will
6 not be repealed or amended in a manner that will adversely affect
7 the imposition or collection of the tax imposed under this chapter
8 if the payment of any of the obligations is outstanding.
9 Sec. 11. (a) If the adopting body imposes the tax authorized by
10 this chapter, the tax terminates on January 1, 2049.
11 (b) This chapter expires January 1, 2049.
12 SECTION 200. IC 8-22-3.5-9, AS AMENDED BY P.L.174-2022,
13 SECTION 50, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
14 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 9. (a) As used in this section, "base assessed
15 value" means, subject to subsection (k):
16 (1) the net assessed value of all the tangible property as finally
17 determined for the assessment date immediately preceding the
18 effective date of the allocation provision of the commission's
19 resolution adopted under section 5 or 9.5 of this chapter,
20 notwithstanding the date of the final action taken under section 6
21 of this chapter; plus
22 (2) to the extent it is not included in subdivision (1), the net
23 assessed value of property that is assessed as residential property
24 under the rules of the department of local government finance,
25 within the airport development zone, as finally determined for the
26 current assessment date.
27 However, subdivision (2) applies only to an airport development zone
28 established after June 30, 1997, and the portion of an airport
29 development zone established before June 30, 1997, that is added to an
30 existing airport development zone.
31 (b) A resolution adopted under section 5 of this chapter and
32 confirmed under section 6 of this chapter must include a provision with
33 respect to the allocation and distribution of property taxes for the
34 purposes and in the manner provided in this section.
35 (c) The allocation provision must:
36 (1) apply to the entire airport development zone; and
37 (2) require that any property tax on taxable tangible property
38 subsequently levied by or for the benefit of any public body
39 entitled to a distribution of property taxes in the airport
40 development zone be allocated and distributed as provided in
41 subsections (d) and (e).
42 (d) Except as otherwise provided in this section:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
233
1 (1) the proceeds of the taxes attributable to the lesser of:
2 (A) the assessed value of the tangible property for the
3 assessment date with respect to which the allocation and
4 distribution is made; or
5 (B) the base assessed value;
6 shall be allocated and, when collected, paid into the funds of the
7 respective taxing units; and
8 (2) the excess of the proceeds of the property taxes imposed for
9 the assessment date with respect to which the allocation and
10 distribution are made that are attributable to taxes imposed after
11 being approved by the voters in a referendum or local public
12 question conducted after April 30, 2010, not otherwise included
13 in subdivision (1) shall be allocated to and, when collected, paid
14 into the funds of the taxing unit for which the referendum or local
15 public question was conducted.
16 (e) All of the property tax proceeds in excess of those described in
17 subsection (d) shall be allocated to the eligible entity for the airport
18 development zone and, when collected, paid into special funds as
19 follows:
20 (1) The commission may determine that a portion of tax proceeds
21 shall be allocated to a training grant fund to be expended by the
22 commission without appropriation solely for the purpose of
23 reimbursing training expenses incurred by public or private
24 entities in the training of employees for the qualified airport
25 development project.
26 (2) The commission may determine that a portion of tax proceeds
27 shall be allocated to a debt service fund and dedicated to the
28 payment of principal and interest on revenue bonds or a loan
29 contract of the board of aviation commissioners or airport
30 authority for a qualified airport development project, to the
31 payment of leases for a qualified airport development project, or
32 to the payment of principal and interest on bonds issued by an
33 eligible entity to pay for qualified airport development projects in
34 the airport development zone or serving the airport development
35 zone.
36 (3) The commission may determine that a part of the tax proceeds
37 shall be allocated to a project fund and used to pay expenses
38 incurred by the commission for a qualified airport development
39 project that is in the airport development zone or is serving the
40 airport development zone.
41 (4) Except as provided in subsection (f), all remaining tax
42 proceeds after allocations are made under subdivisions (1), (2),
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
234
1 and (3) shall be allocated to a project fund and dedicated to the
2 reimbursement of expenditures made by the commission for a
3 qualified airport development project that is in the airport
4 development zone or is serving the airport development zone.
5 (f) Before July 15 of each year, the commission shall do the
6 following:
7 (1) Determine the amount, if any, by which tax proceeds allocated
8 to the project fund in subsection (e)(3) in the following year will
9 exceed the amount necessary to satisfy amounts required under
10 subsection (e).
11 (2) Provide a written notice to the county auditor and the officers
12 who are authorized to fix budgets, tax rates, and tax levies under
13 IC 6-1.1-17-5 for each of the other taxing units that is wholly or
14 partly located within the allocation area. The notice must:
15 (A) state the amount, if any, of excess tax proceeds that the
16 commission has determined may be allocated to the respective
17 taxing units in the manner prescribed in subsection (d)(1); or
18 (B) state that the commission has determined that there are no
19 excess tax proceeds that may be allocated to the respective
20 taxing units in the manner prescribed in subsection (d)(1).
21 The county auditor shall allocate to the respective taxing units the
22 amount, if any, of excess tax proceeds determined by the
23 commission.
24 (g) When money in the debt service fund and in the project fund is
25 sufficient to pay all outstanding principal and interest (to the earliest
26 date on which the obligations can be redeemed) on revenue bonds
27 issued by the board of aviation commissioners or airport authority for
28 the financing of qualified airport development projects, all lease rentals
29 payable on leases of qualified airport development projects, and all
30 costs and expenditures associated with all qualified airport
31 development projects, money in the debt service fund and in the project
32 fund in excess of those amounts shall be paid to the respective taxing
33 units in the manner prescribed by subsection (d)(1).
34 (h) Property tax proceeds allocable to the debt service fund under
35 subsection (e)(2) must, subject to subsection (g), be irrevocably
36 pledged by the eligible entity for the purpose set forth in subsection
37 (e)(2).
38 (i) Notwithstanding any other law, each assessor shall, upon petition
39 of the commission, reassess the taxable tangible property situated upon
40 or in, or added to, the airport development zone effective on the next
41 assessment date after the petition.
42 (j) Notwithstanding any other law, the assessed value of all taxable
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
235
1 tangible property in the airport development zone, for purposes of tax
2 limitation, property tax replacement, and formulation of the budget, tax
3 rate, and tax levy for each political subdivision in which the property
4 is located is the lesser of:
5 (1) the assessed value of the tangible property as valued without
6 regard to this section; or
7 (2) the base assessed value.
8 (k) If the commission confirms, or modifies and confirms, a
9 resolution under section 6 of this chapter and the commission makes
10 either of the filings required under section 6(c) of this chapter after the
11 first anniversary of the effective date of the allocation provision, the
12 auditor of the county in which the airport development zone is located
13 shall compute the base assessed value for the allocation area using the
14 assessment date immediately preceding the later of:
15 (1) the date on which the documents are filed with the county
16 auditor; or
17 (2) the date on which the documents are filed with the department
18 of local government finance.
19 (l) For an airport development zone established after June 30, 2024,
20 "residential property" refers to the assessed value of property that is
21 allocated to the one percent (1%) homestead land and improvement
22 categories in the county tax and billing software system. along with the
23 residential assessed value as defined for purposes of calculating the
24 rate for the local income tax property tax relief credit designated for
25 residential property under IC 6-3.6-5-6(d)(3).

40 (B) "Assignor" means a person that (i) under a security
41 agreement creates or provides for a security interest that
42 secures an obligation or (ii) sells an account, chattel paper,
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
250
1 payment intangible, or promissory note. The term includes a
2 secured party that has transferred a security interest to another
3 person.
4 (8) "Bank" means an organization that is engaged in the business
5 of banking. The term includes savings banks, savings and loan
6 associations, credit unions, and trust companies.
7 (9) "Cash proceeds" means proceeds that are money, checks,
8 deposit accounts, or the like.
9 (10) "Certificate of title" means a certificate of title with respect
10 to which a statute provides for the security interest in question to
11 be indicated on the certificate as a condition or result of the
12 security interest's obtaining priority over the rights of a lien
13 creditor with respect to the collateral. The term includes another
14 record maintained as an alternative to a certificate of title by the
15 governmental unit that issues certificates of title if a statute
16 permits the security interest in question to be indicated on the
17 record as a condition or result of the security interest's obtaining
18 priority over the rights of a lien creditor with respect to the
19 collateral.
20 (11) "Chattel paper" means:
21 (A) a right to payment of a monetary obligation secured by
22 specific goods, if the right to payment and security interest are
23 evidenced by a record; or
24 (B) a right to payment of a monetary obligation owed by a
25 lessee under a lease agreement with respect to specific goods
26 and a monetary obligation owed by the lessee in connection
27 with the transaction giving rise to the lease if:
28 (i) the right to payment and lease agreement are evidenced
29 by a record; and
30 (ii) the predominant purpose of the transaction giving rise to
31 the lease was to give the lessee the right to possession and
32 use of the goods.
33 The term does not include a right to payment arising out of a
34 charter or other contract involving the use or hire of a vessel, or
35 a right to payment arising out of the use of a credit or charge card
36 or information contained on or for use with the card.
37 (12) "Collateral" means the property subject to a security interest
38 or agricultural lien. The term includes:
39 (A) proceeds to which a security interest attaches;
40 (B) accounts, chattel paper, payment intangibles, and
41 promissory notes that have been sold; and
42 (C) goods that are the subject of a consignment.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
251
1 (13) "Commercial tort claim" means a claim arising in tort with
2 respect to which:
3 (A) the claimant is an organization; or
4 (B) the claimant is an individual and the claim:
5 (i) arose in the course of the claimant's business or
6 profession; and
7 (ii) does not include damages arising out of personal injury
8 to or the death of an individual.
9 (14) "Commodity account" means an account maintained by a
10 commodity intermediary in which a commodity contract is carried
11 for a commodity customer.
12 (15) "Commodity contract" means a commodity futures contract,
13 an option on a commodity futures contract, a commodity option,
14 or another contract if the contract or option is:
15 (A) traded on or subject to the rules of a board of trade that has
16 been designated as a contract market for such a contract
17 pursuant to federal commodities laws; or
18 (B) traded on a foreign commodity board of trade, exchange,
19 or market, and is carried on the books of a commodity
20 intermediary for a commodity customer.
21 (16) "Commodity customer" means a person for which a
22 commodity intermediary carries a commodity contract on its
23 books.
24 (17) "Commodity intermediary" means a person that:
25 (A) is registered as a futures commission merchant under
26 federal commodities law; or
27 (B) in the ordinary course of its business provides clearance or
28 settlement services for a board of trade that has been
29 designated as a contract market pursuant to federal
30 commodities law.
31 (18) "Communicate" means:
32 (A) to send a written or other tangible record;
33 (B) to transmit a record by any means agreed upon by the
34 persons sending and receiving the record; or
35 (C) in the case of transmission of a record to or by a filing
36 office, to transmit a record by any means prescribed by
37 filing-office rule.
38 (19) "Consignee" means a merchant to which goods are delivered
39 in a consignment.
40 (20) "Consignment" means a transaction, regardless of its form,
41 in which a person delivers goods to a merchant for the purpose of
42 sale and:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
252
1 (A) the merchant:
2 (i) deals in goods of that kind under a name other than the
3 name of the person making delivery;
4 (ii) is not an auctioneer; and
5 (iii) is not generally known by its creditors to be
6 substantially engaged in selling the goods of others;
7 (B) with respect to each delivery, the aggregate value of the
8 goods is one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more at the time of
9 delivery;
10 (C) the goods are not consumer goods immediately before
11 delivery; and
12 (D) the transaction does not create a security interest that
13 secures an obligation.
14 (21) "Consignor" means a person that delivers goods to a
15 consignee in a consignment.
16 (22) "Consumer debtor" means a debtor in a consumer
17 transaction.
18 (23) "Consumer goods" means goods that are used or bought for
19 use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
20 (24) "Consumer-goods transaction" means a consumer transaction
21 in which:
22 (A) an individual incurs an obligation primarily for personal,
23 family, or household purposes; and
24 (B) a security interest in consumer goods secures the
25 obligation.
26 (25) "Consumer obligor" means an obligor who is an individual
27 and who incurred the obligation as part of a transaction entered
28 into primarily for personal, family, or household purposes.
29 (26) "Consumer transaction" means a transaction in which (i) an
30 individual incurs an obligation primarily for personal, family, or
31 household purposes, (ii) a security interest secures the obligation,
32 and (iii) the collateral is held or acquired primarily for personal,
33 family, or household purposes. The term includes
34 consumer-goods transactions.

33 SECTION 224. IC 32-21-5-7, AS AMENDED BY P.L.186-2025,
34 SECTION 166, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
35 [EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 7. (a) The Indiana real estate
36 commission established by IC 25-34.1-2-1 shall adopt a specific
37 disclosure form that contains the following:
38 (1) Disclosure by the owner of the known condition of the
39 following:
40 (A) The foundation.
41 (B) The mechanical systems.
42 (C) The roof.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
262
1 (D) The structure.
2 (E) The water and sewer systems.
3 (F) Additions that may require improvements to the sewage
4 disposal system.
5 (G) Other areas that the Indiana real estate commission
6 determines are appropriate.
7 (2) Disclosure by the owner of known:
8 (A) contamination caused by the manufacture of a controlled
9 substance (as defined by IC 35-48-1.1-7) on the property that
10 has not been certified as decontaminated by a qualified
11 inspector who is certified under IC 16-19-3.1; or
12 (B) manufacture of methamphetamine or dumping of waste
13 from the manufacture of methamphetamine in a residential
14 structure on the property.
15 (3) A notice to the prospective buyer that contains substantially
16 the following language:
17 "The prospective buyer and the owner may wish to obtain
18 professional advice or inspections of the property and provide for
19 appropriate provisions in a contract between them concerning any
20 advice, inspections, defects, or warranties obtained on the
21 property.".
22 (4) A notice to the prospective buyer that contains substantially
23 the following language:
24 "The representations in this form are the representations of the
25 owner and are not the representations of the agent, if any. This
26 information is for disclosure only and is not intended to be a part
27 of any contract between the buyer and owner.".
28 (5) A disclosure by the owner that an airport is located within a
29 geographical distance from the property as determined by the
30 Indiana real estate commission. The commission may consider the
31 differences between an airport serving commercial airlines and an
32 airport that does not serve commercial airlines in determining the
33 distance to be disclosed.
34 (6) A disclosure by the owner that:
35 (A) the property is located near a military installation, within
36 a state area of interest (as defined in IC 36-7-30.2-6), and may
37 be impacted to some degree by the effects of the installation's
38 military operations; and
39 (B) local laws may restrict use and development of the
40 property to promote compatibility with military installation
41 operations.
42 (7) If the owner has personal knowledge of the fact that all or a
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
263
1 portion of the real estate is located within a community's flood
2 plain boundaries, as indicated in a Federal Emergency
3 Management Agency Flood Insurance Rate Map, a disclosure by
4 the owner of that fact.
5 (8) A disclosure by the owner that the property is located within
6 a locally designated historic district under IC 36-7-11.
7 (9) A disclosure by the owner of a conservation easement (as
8 defined in IC 32-23-5-2).
9 (10) A disclosure by the owner if the property has a lien
10 pursuant to a written agreement with a redevelopment
11 commission or reuse authority under any of the following:
12 (A) IC 8-22-3.5-9.8(b).
13 (B) IC 36-7-14-39.6(b).
14 (C) IC 36-7-15.1-26.6(b).
15 (D) IC 36-7-30-26.5(b).
16 (E) IC 36-7-30.5-31.5(b).
17 (b) Responsibility for the disclosure required under subsection
18 (a)(6) rests solely with the owner of the property and no liability for the
19 owner's failure to make the required disclosure shall accrue to any third
20 party. Failure of the owner to make the required disclosure under
21 subsection (a)(6) shall not:
22 (1) invalidate the transfer of the property; or
23 (2) create any encumbrance or lien upon any legal or equitable
24 title to the property.

27 this chapter shall be determined as follows:
28 STEP ONE: Determine that part of the sum of the amounts
29 described in IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(1)(A) and IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(2)
30 through IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(5) (before their repeal) that is
31 attributable to the taxing district.
32 STEP TWO: Divide:
33 (A) that part of each county's eligible property tax replacement
34 amount (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2) (before its repeal) for
35 that year as determined under IC 6-1.1-21-4(a)(1) (before its
36 repeal) that is attributable to the taxing district; by
37 (B) the amount determined under STEP ONE.
38 STEP THREE: Multiply:
39 (A) the STEP TWO quotient; by
40 (B) the taxpayer's taxes (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2) (before
41 its repeal) levied in the taxing district allocated to the
42 allocation fund, including the amount that would have been
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
288
1 allocated but for the credit.
2 (d) The commission may determine to grant to taxpayers in an
3 allocation area from its allocation fund a credit under this section, as
4 calculated under subsection (c). Except as provided in subsection (g),
5 one-half (1/2) of the credit shall be applied to each installment of taxes
6 (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2) (before its repeal) that under
7 IC 6-1.1-22-9 are due and payable in a year. The commission must
8 provide for the credit annually by a resolution and must find in the
9 resolution the following:
10 (1) That the money to be collected and deposited in the allocation
11 fund, based upon historical collection rates, after granting the
12 credit will equal the amounts payable for contractual obligations
13 from the fund, plus ten percent (10%) of those amounts.
14 (2) If bonds payable from the fund are outstanding, that there is
15 a debt service reserve for the bonds that at least equals the amount
16 of the credit to be granted.
17 (3) If bonds of a lessor under section 25.2 of this chapter or under
18 IC 36-1-10 are outstanding and if lease rentals are payable from
19 the fund, that there is a debt service reserve for those bonds that
20 at least equals the amount of the credit to be granted.
21 If the tax increment is insufficient to grant the credit in full, the
22 commission may grant the credit in part, prorated among all taxpayers.
23 (e) Notwithstanding section 39(b) of this chapter, the allocation
24 fund established under section 39(b) of this chapter for the allocation
25 area for a program adopted under section 45 of this chapter may only
26 be used to do one (1) or more of the following:
27 (1) Accomplish one (1) or more of the actions set forth in section
28 39(b)(4)(A) through 39(b)(4)(H) and 39(b)(4)(J) of this chapter
29 for property that is residential in nature.
30 (2) Reimburse the county or municipality for expenditures made
31 by the county or municipality in order to accomplish the housing
32 program in that allocation area.
33 The allocation fund may not be used for operating expenses of the
34 commission.
35 (f) Notwithstanding section 39(b) of this chapter, the commission
36 shall, relative to the allocation fund established under section 39(b) of
37 this chapter for an allocation area for a program adopted under section
38 45 of this chapter, do the following before June 15 of each year:
39 (1) Determine the amount, if any, by which the assessed value of
40 the taxable property in the allocation area for the most recent

42 by the estimated tax rate of the allocation area, will exceed the

19 (b)(1) and (b)(2) from property located in the enterprise zone that
20 exceeds the amount sufficient for the purposes specified in subsection
21 (b)(3) for the year. A unit that has no obligations, bonds, or leases
22 payable from allocated tax proceeds under subsection (b)(3) shall
23 establish a special zone fund and deposit all the property tax proceeds
24 in excess of those described in subsection (b)(1) and (b)(2) in the fund
25 derived from property tax proceeds in excess of those described in
26 subsection (b)(1) and (b)(2) from property located in the enterprise
27 zone. The unit that creates the special zone fund shall use the fund,
28 based on the recommendations of the urban enterprise association, for
29 one (1) or more of the following purposes:
30 (1) To pay for programs in job training, job enrichment, and basic
31 skill development designed to benefit residents and employers in
32 the enterprise zone. The programs must reserve at least one-half
33 (1/2) of the enrollment in any session for residents of the
34 enterprise zone.
35 (2) To make loans and grants for the purpose of stimulating
36 business activity in the enterprise zone or providing employment
37 for enterprise zone residents in the enterprise zone. These loans
38 and grants may be made to the following:
39 (A) Businesses operating in the enterprise zone.
40 (B) Businesses that will move their operations to the enterprise
41 zone if such a loan or grant is made.
42 (3) To provide funds to carry out other purposes specified in
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
301
1 subsection (b)(3). However, where reference is made in

16 families to purchase or lease residential units within the allocation
17 area. However, financial assistance may be provided only to those
18 individuals and families whose income is at or below the county's
19 median income for individuals and families, respectively.
20 (6) To provide financial assistance to neighborhood development
21 corporations to permit them to provide financial assistance for the
22 purposes described in subdivision (5).
23 (7) For property taxes first due and payable before 2009, to
24 provide each taxpayer in the allocation area a credit for property
25 tax replacement as determined under subsections (c) and (d).
26 However, this credit may be provided by the commission only if
27 the city-county legislative body establishes the credit by
28 ordinance adopted in the year before the year in which the credit
29 is provided.
30 (c) The maximum credit that may be provided under subsection
31 (b)(7) to a taxpayer in a taxing district that contains all or part of an
32 allocation area established for a program adopted under section 32 of
33 this chapter shall be determined as follows:
34 STEP ONE: Determine that part of the sum of the amounts
35 described in IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(1)(A) and IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(2)
36 through IC 6-1.1-21-2(g)(5) (before their repeal) that is
37 attributable to the taxing district.
38 STEP TWO: Divide:
39 (A) that part of each county's eligible property tax replacement
40 amount (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2 (before its repeal)) for
41 that year as determined under IC 6-1.1-21-4(a)(1) (before its
42 repeal) that is attributable to the taxing district; by
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
314
1 (B) the amount determined under STEP ONE.
2 STEP THREE: Multiply:
3 (A) the STEP TWO quotient; by
4 (B) the taxpayer's taxes (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2 (before its
5 repeal)) levied in the taxing district allocated to the allocation
6 fund, including the amount that would have been allocated but
7 for the credit.
8 (d) Except as provided in subsection (g), the commission may
9 determine to grant to taxpayers in an allocation area from its allocation
10 fund a credit under this section, as calculated under subsection (c), by
11 applying one-half (1/2) of the credit to each installment of taxes (as
12 defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2 (before its repeal)) that under IC 6-1.1-22-9
13 are due and payable in a year. Except as provided in subsection (g),
14 one-half (1/2) of the credit shall be applied to each installment of taxes
15 (as defined in IC 6-1.1-21-2 (before its repeal)). The commission must
16 provide for the credit annually by a resolution and must find in the
17 resolution the following:
18 (1) That the money to be collected and deposited in the allocation
19 fund, based upon historical collection rates, after granting the
20 credit will equal the amounts payable for contractual obligations
21 from the fund, plus ten percent (10%) of those amounts.
22 (2) If bonds payable from the fund are outstanding, that there is
23 a debt service reserve for the bonds that at least equals the amount
24 of the credit to be granted.
25 (3) If bonds of a lessor under section 17.1 of this chapter or under
26 IC 36-1-10 are outstanding and if lease rentals are payable from
27 the fund, that there is a debt service reserve for those bonds that
28 at least equals the amount of the credit to be granted.
29 If the tax increment is insufficient to grant the credit in full, the
30 commission may grant the credit in part, prorated among all taxpayers.
31 (e) Notwithstanding section 26(b) of this chapter, the special fund
32 established under section 26(b) of this chapter for the allocation area
33 for a program adopted under section 32 of this chapter may only be
34 used to do one (1) or more of the following:
35 (1) Accomplish one (1) or more of the actions set forth in section
36 26(b)(3)(A) through 26(b)(3)(H) of this chapter.
37 (2) Reimburse the consolidated city for expenditures made by the
38 city in order to accomplish the housing program in that allocation
39 area.
40 The special fund may not be used for operating expenses of the
41 commission.
42 (f) Notwithstanding section 26(b) of this chapter, the commission
