EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
644

Section 494:
SECTION 105, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
[EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 15. (a) As used in this section,

payment of debt service on bonds used to implement solutions to a
contractual retirement or severance liability.

outstanding.

corporation must include in its request a copy of the ordinance adopted
under IC 5-1-5-2.5 or IC 5-1-5-2.6.

school corporation permission to continue to impose such a debt
service fund levy if the department finds that the school corporation
qualifies to issue refunding bonds under IC 5-1-5-2.5 or IC 5-1-5-2.6.

impose a debt service fund levy as described in this section may
transfer the lesser of the amount of credits granted under IC 6-1.1-20.6
against the school corporation's combined levy for all the school
corporation's funds or the amount of the increment from the debt

647
(A) IC 8-22-3.5-9.8(b).

(E) IC 36-7-30.5-31.5(b).
(b) Responsibility for the disclosure required under subsection
(a)(6) rests solely with the owner of the property and no liability for the
owner's failure to make the required disclosure shall accrue to any third
party. Failure of the owner to make the required disclosure under
subsection (a)(6) shall not:
(1) invalidate the transfer of the property; or
(2) create any encumbrance or lien upon any legal or equitable
title to the property.

Section 498:
SECTION 142. IC 32-25.5-3-11, AS ADDED BY P.L.27-2017,

Section 499:
SECTION 3, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 11. (a) If:
(1) a meeting of a homeowners association is called in accordance
with the requirements of the homeowners association's governing
documents, regardless of whether the meeting is:
(A) an annual meeting;
(B) a special meeting; or
(C) any other meeting called by the board or the members;
(2) a purpose of the meeting is the election or appointment of
members of the board of directors of the homeowners association;
and
(3) the number of members of the homeowners association in
attendance at the meeting does not constitute a quorum as defined
in the governing documents of the homeowners association;
the members of the board of directors at the time of the meeting may
continue to serve until their successors are selected and qualified,
regardless of the length of any member's term or the number of terms
the member has served.
(b) The failure of a homeowners association to achieve a quorum at
a meeting described in subsection (a), or the fact that a member is
not eligible to serve on the board of directors or vote in the election
of the board of directors under subsection (d), does not exempt any
member from, or create an affirmative defense for any member with
respect to:
(1) the member's obligations under the homeowners association's
governing documents; or
(2) the member's obligations to otherwise abide by covenants
regulating:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
648
(A) the use of real estate; or
(B) the payment of assessments.
(c) If a homeowners association's governing documents permit both
the homeowners association and members of the homeowners
association to enforce provisions of the governing documents, the
homeowners association has authority both:
(1) as a corporation or an entity; and
(2) as derived from the members of the homeowners association's
board;
to enforce the governing documents of the homeowners association.
(d) Beginning after the effective date of this subsection as added
by HEA 1210-2026, only members of the homeowners association
who use their property as a homestead (as defined in
IC 6-1.1-12-37) are eligible to:
(1) be elected and serve as a member of the board of directors
of the homeowners association;
(2) cast a vote for a member of the homeowners association
who is eligible to be a member of the board of directors of the
homeowners association; or
(3) cast a vote on a matter regarding any of the following:
(A) A prohibition or restriction of an owner of a privately
owned residential property from using the property as a
rental property.
(B) A prohibition or restriction regarding the use of
property as a rental property.
The term of a member of the board of directors of a homeowners
association who does not meet the qualification under this section
terminates on the effective date of this subsection as added by HEA
1210-2026.".

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
649
(1) a single family home;
(2) a dwelling unit in a single family home;

that is operated primarily as a business.

"SECTION 149. IC 36-7-14-39.6, AS ADDED BY P.L.249-2015,

Section 504:
SECTION 26, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 39.6. (a) A redevelopment commission may enter
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
651
into a written agreement with a taxpayer who owns, or is otherwise
obligated to pay property taxes on, tangible property that is or will be
located in an allocation area established under this chapter in which the
taxpayer waives review of any assessment of the taxpayer's tangible
property that is located in the allocation area for an assessment date
that occurs during the term of any specified bond or lease obligations
that are payable from property taxes in accordance with an allocation
provision for the allocation area and any applicable statute, ordinance,
or resolution. An agreement described in this section may precede the
establishment of the allocation area or the determination to issue bonds
or enter into leases payable from the allocated property taxes.
(b) The original owner of each nonowner occupied residential
property subject to the two percent (2%) tax cap, that is located in
the tax increment financing area and is excluded from the base
assessed value, shall upon completion of construction enter into a
written agreement with the redevelopment commission indicating
the owner shall be obligated to pay the property tax for the portion
of outstanding bonds in the tax increment financing district
attributable to the property until the term length of the original
outstanding bond is retired. The written agreement with the
redevelopment commission shall be considered a lien on the
property and shall be included as part of the residential real estate
sales disclosure under IC 32-21-5. If the property is subsequently
sold as a homestead property and becomes subject to the one
percent (1%) tax cap, the new owner shall be responsible for the
lien on the property attributable to the written agreement with the
redevelopment commission, and the new homestead property
owner shall be obligated to fulfill the terms of the written
agreement including the payment of the property tax liability
included in the agreement.

[EFFECTIVE UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 12.1 (a) This section applies

(b) This section is an alternative to section 12 of this chapter if
the appointment of the governing board under section 12 of this
chapter would exceed nine (9) members. The county legislative
body may instead opt to appoint the governing board under this

Section 519:
section.
(c) The board of trustees appointed under this section is
composed of nine (9) members appointed as follows:

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

appointment for a term of two (2) years.
(3) Two (2) county commissioner appointments and one (1)
county council appointment for a term of three (3) years.
(e) The terms expire on the first Monday of January of the year
their appointments expire. As the terms expire, each new
appointment is for a term of four (4) years.
(f) If a vacancy occurs on the board, the county legislative body
shall appoint a trustee with the qualifications specified in section
12(b) of this chapter for the unexpired term. Members appointed
must comply with section 12 of this chapter.

Section 520:
SECTION 181. IC 36-8-11-16 IS AMENDED TO READ AS
FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]:
Sec. 16. (a) All the real property within a fire protection district
constitutes a taxing district for the purpose of levying taxes to pay for
the construction, operation, and maintenance of district programs and
facilities. A tax levied must be levied at a uniform rate upon all taxable
property within the district. A fire protection district is a municipal
corporation within the meaning of the Constitution of Indiana and all
general statutes.
(b) This subsection applies to a fire protection district
established by an ordinance or a resolution adopted under this
chapter after December 31, 2025. The district may not impose a tax
rate on the taxable property within the district that exceeds forty
cents ($0.40) per one hundred dollars ($100) of assessed valuation.

Section 521:
SECTION 173. IC 36-8-12-16, AS AMENDED BY P.L.186-2025,

Section 522:
SECTION 258, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
[EFFECTIVE JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 16. (a) A volunteer fire department
that provides service within a jurisdiction served by the department
may establish a schedule of charges for the services that the department
provides not to exceed the state fire marshal's recommended schedule
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
659
for services. The volunteer fire department or its agent may collect a
service charge according to this schedule from the owner of property
that receives service if the following conditions are met:
(1) At the following times, the department gives notice under
IC 5-3-1-4(d) IC 5-3-1-1.5 in each political subdivision served by
the department of the amount of the service charge for each
service that the department provides:
(A) Before the schedule of service charges is initiated.
(B) When there is a change in the amount of a service charge.
(2) The property owner has not sent written notice to the
department to refuse service by the department to the owner's
property.
(3) The bill for payment of the service charge:
(A) is submitted to the property owner in writing within thirty
(30) days after the services are provided;
(B) includes a copy of a fire incident report in the form
prescribed by the state fire marshal, if the service was
provided for an event that requires a fire incident report;
(C) must contain verification that the bill has been approved
by the chief of the volunteer fire department; and
(D) must contain language indicating that correspondence
from the property owner and any question from the property
owner regarding the bill should be directed to the department.
(4) Payment is remitted directly to the governmental unit
providing the service.
(b) A volunteer fire department shall use the revenue collected from
the fire service charges under this section:
(1) for the purchase of equipment, buildings, and property for
firefighting, fire protection, or other emergency services;
(2) for deposit in the township firefighting and emergency
services fund established under IC 36-8-13-4(a)(1) or the
township firefighting fund established under
IC 36-8-13-4(a)(2)(A); or
(3) to pay principal and interest on a loan made by the department
of homeland security established by IC 10-19-2-1 or a division of
the department for the purchase of new or used firefighting and
other emergency equipment or apparatus.
(c) Any administrative fees charged by a fire department's agent
must be paid only from fees that are collected and allowed by Indiana
law and the fire marshal's schedule of fees.
(d) An agent who processes fees on behalf of a fire department shall
send all bills, notices, and other related materials to both the fire
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
660
department and the person being billed for services.
(e) All fees allowed by Indiana law and the fire marshal's fee
schedule must be itemized separately from any other charges.
(f) If at least twenty-five percent (25%) of the money received by a
volunteer fire department for providing fire protection or emergency
services is received under one (1) or more contracts with one (1) or
more political subdivisions (as defined in IC 34-6-2.1-155), the
legislative body of a contracting political subdivision must approve the
schedule of service charges established under subsection (a) before the
schedule of service charges is initiated in that political subdivision.
(g) A volunteer fire department that:
(1) has contracted with a political subdivision to provide fire
protection or emergency services; and
(2) charges for services under this section;
must submit a report to the legislative body of the political subdivision
before April 1 of each year indicating the amount of service charges
collected during the previous calendar year and how those funds have
been expended.
(h) The state fire marshal shall annually prepare and publish a
recommended schedule of service charges for fire protection services.
(i) The volunteer fire department or its agent may maintain a civil
action to recover an unpaid service charge under this section and may,
if it prevails, recover all costs of the action, including reasonable
attorney's fees.

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
669
(b) The county assessor of a qualifying county may not expend

following requirements are met before a petition for review is
submitted to the Indiana board:
(1) The county assessor submits to the county fiscal body a

(2) The county fiscal body adopts a resolution approving the
county assessor's proposed expenditure to carry out the appeal.
(3) The total amount of the proposed expenditure is in accordance
with an appropriation made by the county fiscal body in the
manner provided by law.
(c) (b) Except as otherwise provided in subsections (d), and (e),
subsection (c), an appeal under this section shall be conducted in the

An assessment made under this chapter that is not appealed under this

Section 533:
section is a final unappealable order of the department of local
government finance.
(d) With respect to an appeal filed by a county assessor under this

Section 534:
section the following apply:
(1) In the petition for review to the Indiana board, the county
assessor shall state what the county assessor contends the
assessed value of the industrial facility should be and provide
substantial evidence in support of that contention. Failure to
comply with this requirement results in dismissal of the county
assessor's petition for review and no further appeal of the
assessment by the county assessor may be taken.
(2) Not later than thirty (30) days after the county assessor files a
petition for review in compliance with subdivision (1), the
Indiana board shall hold a hearing at which the county assessor
must establish a reasonable likelihood of success on any
contentions made in the petition for review including, without
limitation, the contention required under subdivision (1)
regarding the assessed value of the real estate. The industrial
company whose industrial facility is the subject of the county
assessor's petition for review and the department of local
government finance has the right to appear at this hearing and to
present testimony, to cross-examine witnesses, and to present
evidence regarding the county assessor's contentions.
(3) Not later than thirty (30) days after the hearing held under
subdivision (2), the Indiana board shall issue a determination
whether the county assessor has established a reasonable
likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition for review.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
670
If the Indiana board determines that the county assessor has not
established a reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions
in the petition for review, the county assessor's petition for review
shall be dismissed and no further appeal of the assessment by the
county assessor may be taken. If the Indiana board determines that
the county assessor has established a reasonable likelihood of
success on the contentions in the petition for review, the Indiana
board's determination does not create the presumption that the
county assessor's contentions are valid. A determination by the
Indiana board that the county assessor has established a
reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition
for review may be appealed to the Indiana tax court as an
interlocutory appeal. A party may petition for review by the
Indiana supreme court of the Indiana tax court's ruling regarding
an interlocutory appeal brought under this subdivision.
(4) The Indiana board shall not hold a hearing on the appeal under
IC 6-1.1-15-4 and the county assessor shall not be permitted to
conduct discovery under the Indiana board's administrative rules
until a determination has been issued under subdivision (3) and:
(A) any interlocutory appeal under subdivision (3) has been
ruled on by the Indiana tax court; or
(B) the Indiana supreme court has either rejected a petition for
review concerning the Indiana tax court's ruling on the
interlocutory appeal or issued a decision regarding the Indiana
tax court's ruling on the interlocutory appeal.
(e) (c) On any appeal that has not been dismissed, the Indiana board
shall issue an order within one (1) year two (2) years after
(1) the taxpayer filed its petition for review.
(2) the issuance of the Indiana board's determination under
subsection (d)(3) in the case of an appeal by the county assessor;
or
(3) the Indiana tax court or Indiana supreme court rules on a
taxpayer's interlocutory appeal under subsection (d)(3) in the case
of an appeal by the county assessor;
whichever is latest.

is established as a dedicated fund to provide money to cover the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
671
costs incurred by the department of local government finance
during an industrial facility assessment appeal.

revert to the state general fund at the end of a state fiscal year.

of the fund.

thousand dollars ($500,000) from the county's reassessment fund
(as defined in IC 6-1.1-4-27.5) before December 31 of each year to
the state comptroller for deposit in the fund. The county fiscal
officer shall stop payments to the fund once the balance of the fund
reaches three million dollars ($3,000,000). However, if the fund
balance is below two million dollars ($2,000,000) then the county
fiscal officer shall continue to deposit the annual five hundred
thousand dollars ($500,000) until the fund reaches three million
dollars ($3,000,000).
(e) The budget agency shall administer the fund.
(f) The budget committee shall review all expenditures from the
fund prior to the release of the funds.

shall be dismissed, and no further appeal of the assessment by the
county assessor may be taken. If the Indiana board determines that
the county assessor has established a reasonable likelihood of
success on the contentions in the petition for review, the Indiana
board's determination does not create the presumption that the
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
673
county assessor's contentions are valid. A determination by the
Indiana board that the county assessor has established a
reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition
for review may be appealed to the Indiana tax court as an
interlocutory appeal. A party may petition for review by the
Indiana supreme court of the Indiana tax court's ruling regarding
an interlocutory appeal brought under this subdivision.
(4) The Indiana board shall not hold a hearing on the appeal under
IC 6-1.1-15-4 and the county assessor shall not be permitted to
conduct discovery under the Indiana board's administrative rules
until a determination has been issued under subdivision (3) and:
(A) any interlocutory appeal under subdivision (3) has been
ruled on by the Indiana tax court; or
(B) the Indiana supreme court has either rejected a petition for
review concerning the Indiana tax court's ruling on the
interlocutory appeal or issued a decision regarding the Indiana
tax court's ruling on the interlocutory appeal.
(e) (c) On any appeal that has not been dismissed, the Indiana board
shall issue an order within one (1) year two (2) years after
(1) the taxpayer filed its petition for review.
(2) the issuance of the Indiana board's determination under
subsection (d)(3) in the case of an appeal by the county assessor;
or
(3) the Indiana tax court or the Indiana supreme court rules on a
taxpayer's interlocutory appeal under subsection (d)(3) in the case
of an appeal by the county assessor;
whichever is latest.

Section 541:
SECTION 45. IC 6-1.1-8.7-11 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA
CODE AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS
[EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 11. (a) As
used in this section, "fund" refers to the assessment of industrial
facilities fund established by subsection (b).
(b) The assessment of industrial facilities fund is established as
a dedicated fund to provide money to cover the costs incurred by
the department of local government finance during an industrial
facility assessment appeal.
(c) Money in the fund and income derived from the fund do not
revert to the state general fund at the end of a state fiscal year.
Money in the fund is continuously appropriated for the purposes
of the fund.
(d) The county fiscal officer shall transfer fifty thousand dollars
($50,000) from the county's reassessment fund (as defined in
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134
674
IC 6-1.1-4-27.5) before December 31 of each year to the state
comptroller for deposit in the fund. The county fiscal officer shall
stop payments to the fund once the balance of the fund reaches
three million dollars ($3,000,000). However, if the fund balance is
below two million dollars ($2,000,000) then the county fiscal officer
shall continue to deposit the annual fifty thousand dollars ($50,000)
until the fund reaches three million dollars ($3,000,000).
(e) The budget agency shall administer the fund.
(f) The budget committee shall review all expenditures from the
fund prior to the release of the funds.".

the population of the most recent decennial census in the city.".

Reprinted
February 20, 2026
ENGROSSED
HOUSE BILL No. 1210
_____
DIGEST OF HB 1210 (Updated February 19, 2026 5:27 pm - DI 129)
Citations Affected: Numerous provisions throughout the Indiana
Code.
Synopsis: Department of local government finance. Requires a
municipal entity that hires or retains a municipal advisor to complete
a request for qualifications at least once every three years to select the
municipal advisor. Requires a municipal advisor to register with the
department of local government finance (DLGF). Provides for the
expiration of certain municipal entity contracts. Modifies the amount
of supplemental wagering tax that the treasurer of state is required to
pay to the riverboat operating in Gary and the distribution of wagering
(Continued next page)
Effective: Upon passage; July 1, 2018 (retroactive); January 1, 2024
(retroactive); January 1, 2025 (retroactive); May 10, 2025 (retroactive);
July 1, 2025 (retroactive); January 1, 2026 (retroactive); July 1, 2026;
July 1, 2027; January 1, 2028; June 30, 2028; July 1, 2028; January 1,
2029.
Snow, Lopez, Slager, Pryor
(SENATE SPONSORS — HOLDMAN, BALDWIN, MISHLER, ROGERS,
RANDOLPH LONNIE M)
January 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Ways and Means.
January 27, 2026, amended, reported — Do Pass.
January 29, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
January 30, 2026, engrossed.
February 2, 2026, read third time, passed. Yeas 82, nays 1.
SENATE ACTION
February 5, 2026, read first time and referred to Committee on Tax and Fiscal Policy.
February 17, 2026, amended, reported favorably — Do Pass.
February 19, 2026, read second time, amended, ordered engrossed.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

Digest Continued
tax revenue to the city of Gary. Provides that the Lake County
Convention Center Authority is established upon the substantial
completion of the convention and event center. Establishes an
alternative procedure for certain school corporations to issue refunding
bonds. Requires state agencies and political subdivisions to cooperate
with the state GIS officer in preparing a statewide base map. Eliminates
the requirement that the DLGF work with the office of technology or
another organization that is part of a state educational institution for
purposes of posting information on the Indiana transparency website
and submitting forms regarding data for local units. Allows a person to
satisfy any notice statute by publishing notice in specified forms of
media and modifies related provisions regarding notice by publication.
Provides that in assessing or reassessing land, the land shall be
assessed as agricultural land regardless of who owns the property or
who is liable for the property taxes. Specifies the burden of proof in
particular circumstances involving land previously assessed as
agricultural land. Amends and adds provisions regarding the
assessment of certain industrial facilities. Modifies procedures as to the
reporting of assessment values of real and personal property and parcel
level data. Changes the deadline for a county to submit to the DLGF
data regarding real property, personal property, and geographic
information system information. Requires the purchaser of a mobile
home to process the paperwork with the bureau of motor vehicles to
transfer the title into the purchaser's name within 90 days of the sale.
Specifies eligibility for certain townships to petition for an increase to
the maximum property tax levy for the firefighting and emergency
services fund. Makes procedural changes for civil taxing units not
subject to levy limits. Provides that certain local property tax credits
result in a reduction of property tax collections in a political
subdivision in which such a credit is applied. Specifies the procedures
for the submission of certain forms and related allocation amounts with
regard to various allocation areas. Amends various tax increment
financing allocation area provisions, including provisions to redefine
"residential property" and to require the original owner of certain
nonowner-occupied residential property in an allocation area to enter
into a written agreement with the appropriate entity to pay the property
taxes for the portion of outstanding bonds until the bonds are retired.
Changes reporting requirements by governing bodies to the DLGF
regarding guaranteed savings contracts and energy efficient programs
used by school corporations. Provides that the property tax rate for the
levy imposed for the replacement of fire protection territory equipment
is considered part of the maximum permissible ad valorem property tax
levy and may not exceed $0.0333 per $100 of assessed value. Extends
a temporary increase in the capitalization rate percentage under the
statewide agricultural land base rate determination. Requires the DLGF
to annually publish on the Indiana Register the adjusted cost estimate
threshold for certain local public work projects. Provides a real and
personal property tax exemption for Indiana nonprofit senior living
communities beginning with property taxes that are first due and
payable in 2027. Adds the the Indiana Historical Society, Inc. to a list
of organizations exempt from property taxation. Allows certain
taxpayers to retroactively file a property tax exemption application.
Increases the number of model residences and residences in inventory
from three to seven that may be claimed for purposes of those property
tax deductions. Requires an individual to reside on the real property,
mobile home, or manufactured home to be eligible for the over 65
property tax credit. Increases the property tax deduction for a veteran
who is totally disabled to 100% of the assessed value of the individual's
real property (instead of $14,000). Expires property tax deductions for
certain veterans, and instead provides a property tax liability credit.
Restores the property tax deduction available to a surviving spouse of
a World War I veteran that was limited by SEA 1-2025. Provides, if a
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344

Digest Continued
taxpayer claims the homestead deduction for property that is not
eligible for the deduction, that the taxpayer shall (instead of may) be
liable for any additional taxes that would have been due on homestead
property plus a civil penalty. Requires the county auditor to include in
a notice of tax due a 10% fine as a penalty for claiming the homestead
deduction falsely, which is in addition to all other penalties for which
the taxpayer is liable. Allows the executive of Miami Township in Cass
County to submit a petition to the DLGF requesting an increase in the
township's maximum permissible ad valorem property tax levy for
property taxes first due and payable in 2027. Requires the Hancock
County fiscal body to adopt a resolution to allow a one time transfer of
money from the library property tax replacement fund. Provides that for
purposes of fixing and reviewing budgets, tax rates, and tax levies,
before a county auditor makes an amendment, the county auditor must
provide written notice to the county fiscal body, the DLGF, and the
fiscal officers of the affected taxing units. Provides that the DLGF may
not approve the budget for a political subdivision until an attestation
statement concerning the uploading of contracts is submitted. Requires
certain qualified data center users to enter into an agreement with local
officials before the qualified data center user may use a specific
transaction award certificate. Adds a provision regarding repayment in
the redevelopment tax credit. Specifies eligibility and procedures for
a health reimbursement arrangement income tax credit. Moves the
effective date for the local income tax changes enacted in SEA 1-2025
from 2028 to 2029. Makes corresponding changes to move the
expiration date regarding a county with a single voting bloc enacted in
HEA 1142-2025. Specifies procedures for the imposition of local
income taxes and distribution of local income tax revenue. Removes
provisions enacted in SEA 1-2025 providing for the expiration of local
income tax expenditure rates for counties or municipalities that fail to
adopt an ordinance to renew an existing expenditure tax rate. Provides
that the county may determine an allocation method for revenue raised
from a tax rate for fire protection or emergency medical services.
Provides that the county and certain township fire departments must
receive an allocation of revenue raised from a tax rate for fire
protection or emergency medical services. Provides a formula for the
distribution of revenue from the local income tax rate imposed by a
county for certain small cities and towns. Specifies procedures for
determining population for purposes of a municipal local income tax
rate. Allows each county to establish a local strategic taskforce to
negotiate and determine certain maximum local income tax rates.
Allows the Marshall County jail fund to be used for costs otherwise
incurred for the operation of the county jail. Provides that distributions
of certain excise tax revenue to a taxing unit may be deposited in any
fund maintained by the taxing unit and may be used for any purpose
allowed by law. Makes numerous changes and additions to the local
innkeeper's tax and food and beverage tax statutes, including new food
and beverage tax authorizations, modifications to distributions of
innkeeper's tax revenue, and revisions to the composition of various
innkeeper's tax boards and commissions. Replaces the definitions of
"manufactured home" and "mobile home" throughout the Indiana Code
with a singular definition. Subject to an exception, limits voting
eligibilty in a homeowners association to members of the homeowners
association who use their property as a homestead as to certain matters.
With certain exceptions, prohibits a unit from adopting or enforcing an
ordinance, resolution, regulation, policy, or rule that prohibits or
restricts an owner of a privately owned residential property from using
the property as a rental property. Retroactively amends the definition
of "short term rental" to specify that certain private, owner occupied
businesses are excluded from that definition. Requires a person
appointed to a fire protection district board of trustees to reside in the
fire protection district. Sets forth an alternative procedure that may be
(Continued next page)
EEHH 11221100——LLSS 66880055//DDII 113344

Digest Continued
used to appoint the board of trustees of certain fire protection districts.
Provides that a fire protection district established after December 31,
2025, may not impose a tax rate within the district that exceeds $0.40
per $100 of assessed valuation. Revises a provision of the municipal
Barrett Law concerning deferred installments. Amends provisions in
the drainage law as to bidding on certain projects and the term of loans.
Authorizes the budget agency, subject to budget committee review, to
augment the state agency contingency fund appropriations in HEA
1001-2025 through July 1, 2027, in an amount not to exceed
$40,000,000 for Indiana office of technology contracts, in addition to
the uses for the appropriation authorized in HEA 1001-2025.

Reprinted
February 20, 2026
Second Regular Session of the 124th General Assembly (2026)
PRINTING CODE. Amendments: Whenever an existing statute (or a section of the Indiana
Constitution) is being amended, the text of the existing provision will appear in this style type,
additions will appear in this style type, and deletions will appear in this style type.
Additions: Whenever a new statutory provision is being enacted (or a new constitutional
provision adopted), the text of the new provision will appear in this style type. Also, the
word NEW will appear in that style type in the introductory clause of each SECTION that adds
a new provision to the Indiana Code or the Indiana Constitution.
Conflict reconciliation: Text in a statute in this style type or this style type reconciles conflicts
between statutes enacted by the 2025 Regular Session of the General Assembly.

A BILL FOR AN ACT to amend the Indiana Code concerning state
and local administration and to make an appropriation.

3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 18. (a) If the election division determines that the
4 proposed precinct establishment order would comply with this chapter,
5 the election division shall issue an order authorizing the county
6 executive to establish the proposed precincts.

8 must state that the election division finds that the proposed precincts
9 would comply with the standards set forth in this chapter. The election
10 division shall promptly provide a copy of the order to the county
11 executive.
12 (c) The county executive must give notice of the proposed order to
13 the voters of the county by one (1) publication under IC 5-3-1-4.

15 (1) The name of each existing precinct whose boundaries would

6 commission under IC 4-21.5.
7 (3) The mailing address of the election division.
8 (4) The deadline for filing the objection with the election division
9 under this section.
10 (d) Except as provided in subsection (g), an objection to a proposed
11 precinct establishment order must be filed not later than noon ten (10)
12 days after the publication of the notice by the county executive.
13 (e) If an objection is not filed with the election division by the date
14 and time specified under subsection (d), the election division shall
15 promptly notify the county executive. The county executive may
16 proceed immediately to adopt the proposed order.

19 promptly notify the county executive. The county executive may not
20 adopt the proposed order until the commission conducts a hearing
21 under IC 4-21.5 and determines whether the proposed precincts would
22 comply with the standards set forth in this chapter.

37 (1) a ballot card voting system; and
38 (2) a voting system that includes features of a ballot card voting

3
1 randomly sorted list of unique identification numbers for the inventory
2 of machines in the county maintained under IC 3-11-16-4. Starting at

5 (1) if a machine to be selected in the list is not scheduled to be
6 used in the upcoming election, the selection process will move to
7 the next machine in the order listed;
8 (2) each selected machine is scheduled to be used in the
9 upcoming election; and
10 (3) the number of machines selected is not less than five percent
11 (5%) of the machines in the county scheduled by the county
12 election board to be used in the upcoming election.
13 (c) The county election board shall test the machines as described

16 (1) Count the votes cast for straight party tickets, for all
17 candidates (including write-in candidates), and on all public
18 questions.
19 (2) Retract an absentee ballot card of a voter who is later found

21 with IC 3-11.5-4-6, before the voter's ballot is tabulated.
22 If an individual attending the public test requests that additional
23 automatic tabulating machines be tested, then the county election board
24 shall select and test additional machines from the list in the manner
25 described in subsection (b).
26 (d) If VSTOP does not provide the lists under subsection (b) not
27 later than sixty (60) days before the election, the county election board
28 shall establish and implement a procedure for random selection of not
29 less than five percent (5%) of the machines in the county to be used in
30 the upcoming election. The county election board shall then test the
31 machines selected as described in subsection (c).

2 SECTION 9, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
3 JULY 1, 2026]: Sec. 8. (a) As used in this section, "audit threshold
4 number" refers to the following number:
5 (1) One (1), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
6 subsection (c), is not more than twenty (20).
7 (2) Two (2), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
8 subsection (c), is:
9 (A) more than twenty (20); but
10 (B) not more than forty (40).
11 (3) Three (3), if the total number of votes cast, as determined
12 under subsection (c), is:
13 (A) more than forty (40); but
14 (B) not more than sixty (60).
15 (4) Four (4), if the total number of votes cast, as determined under
16 subsection (c), is:
17 (A) more than sixty (60); but
18 (B) not more than eighty (80).
19 (5) Five percent (5%) of the total number of votes cast, rounded
20 up to the nearest whole number, if the total number of votes cast,
21 as determined under subsection (c), is:
22 (A) more than eighty (80); but
23 (B) not more than five hundred (500).
24 (6) Twenty-five (25), if the total number of votes cast, as
25 determined under subsection (c) is more than five hundred (500).
26 (b) As used in this section, "judge" refers only to the judge who is
27 a member of a political party other than the political party of the
28 inspector.
29 (c) After each electronic voting system has been secured and the
30 paper vote total printouts obtained, the inspector and judge shall record
31 the total number of:
32 (1) votes cast on all electronic voting systems located within the
33 precinct; and
34 (2) voters who have received a ballot by signing in at the polls
35 according to the poll lists for each precinct;
36 to determine if the total number of votes cast on the electronic voting
37 systems differs from the number of voters shown to have received a
38 ballot at the polls according to the poll lists.
39 (d) The inspector and judge shall record the information set forth in
40 subsection (c) on a form prescribed under IC 3-5-4-8 and provided to
41 each precinct and vote center under IC 3-11-3-10 by the county
42 election board. The inspector and judge shall sign the form before
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

6
1 delivering the certificates in accordance with section 4 of this chapter
2 and return the form with the certificates.
3 (e) If the number of ballots received at the polls differs from the
4 total number of voters shown on the poll lists, the inspector and judge
5 shall report this fact in writing to the county election board together
6 with the reasons for the discrepancy, if known, at the time that the
7 inspector and judge return the precinct poll list to the board on the form
8 required under subsection (d).
9 (f) The county election board shall compile the following
10 information into a single document listing for each precinct:
11 (1) The number of votes cast on the electronic voting systems in
12 the precinct, as shown on the form required for the precinct under
13 subsection (d).
14 (2) The number of voters who cast ballots on the electronic voting
15 systems as shown on the form required for the precinct under
16 subsection (d).
17 (3) The number of absentee ballots returned by voters of the
18 precinct.
19 (4) The number of absentee ballots described in subdivision (3)
20 that were counted.
21 (5) The difference between the number in subdivision (1) and the
22 number in subdivision (2).
23 Not later than noon on the second Friday following the election, the
24 county election board shall discuss and publish the document described
25 in this subsection at a public hearing and immediately make the
26 document available for inspection and copying by any voter of the
27 county.
28 (g) If the number determined under subsection (f)(5) is greater than
29 or equal to the audit threshold number, then the county election board
30 or the secretary of state may order an audit of all the votes cast in that
31 precinct under this section. Before ordering an audit, the county
32 election board shall recheck the computations reported by the inspector
33 and judge under subsection (c).
34 (h) The county election board shall confirm that the votes cast in an
35 election:
36 (1) for each candidate and each public question; and
37 (2) on a direct record electronic voting system in the precinct;
38 were correctly counted.
39 (i) The county election board shall conduct an audit by means of
40 tests and procedures that are approved by the commission and
41 independent of the provider of the direct record electronic voting
42 system being audited.

1 (j) The county election board shall certify the results of the audit not

13 SECTION 14. IC 5-1-5-2, AS AMENDED BY P.L.229-2011,
14 SECTION 62, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
15 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2. (a) The governing body of any issuing body
16 may by ordinance provide for the issuance of bonds to refund
17 outstanding bonds issued at any time by such issuing body or its
18 predecessor, and to pay redemption premiums and costs of refunding
19 to effect a saving to the issuing body. Issuance of bonds to refund
20 outstanding bonds may also be made in order to pay or discharge all or
21 any part of such outstanding series or issue of bond, including any
22 interest thereon, in arrears or about to become due and for which
23 sufficient funds are not available or to modify restrictive covenants in
24 outstanding bonds impeding additional financing. To determine
25 whether or not a savings will be effected, consideration shall be given
26 to the estimated or known interest payable to the fixed maturities of the
27 refunding bonds, the interest payable on the bonds to be refunded, the
28 costs of issuance of the refunding bonds, including any sale discount,
29 the redemption premiums, if any, to be paid, and the probable earned
30 income from the investment of the refunding bond proceeds pending
31 redemption of the bonds to be refunded.
32 (b) The provisions of subsection (a) requiring a savings to be
33 effected do not apply to:
34 (1) the issuance of bonds to refund previously issued refunding
35 bonds, if the statute under which the refunding bonds are issued
36 expressly exempts such an issue from this savings requirement;
37 or
38 (2) the issuance of refunding bonds by a school corporation that
39 is an eligible school corporation under section 2.5 or 2.6 of this
40 chapter.
41 SECTION 15. IC 5-1-5-2.6 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
42 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

28
1 UPON PASSAGE]: Sec. 2.6. (a) This section applies as an
2 alternative to section 2.5 of this chapter.
3 (b) As used in this section, "eligible school corporation" means
4 a school corporation (as defined in IC 36-1-2-17) that satisfies all
5 the conditions required by this section.
6 (c) As used in this section, "increment" means the annual
7 difference between:
8 (1) the annual debt service payment for the bonds proposed to
9 be retired or refunded; and
10 (2) the annual debt service payment for the proposed
11 refunding bonds;
12 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or refunded
13 would have been outstanding.
14 (d) In order for a school corporation to be an eligible school
15 corporation under this section, the school corporation must
16 determine that:
17 (1) the percentage computed under subsection (e) for the
18 school corporation is at least sixty percent (60%); and
19 (2) application of this section will not reduce revenue to other
20 local units in the same county;
21 regarding the year for which the latest certified levies have been
22 determined.
23 (e) A school corporation shall compute its percentage as follows:
24 (1) Compute the amount of credits granted under
25 IC 6-1.1-20.6 against the school corporation's levy for the
26 school corporation's operations fund (IC 20-46-8).
27 (2) Compute the school corporation's levy for the school
28 corporation's operations fund.
29 (3) Divide the amount computed under subdivision (1) by the
30 amount computed under subdivision (2) and express it as a
31 percentage.
32 (f) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
33 corporation under this section must submit a written request for
34 a certification by the department of local government finance that
35 the computation of the school corporation's percentage computed
36 under subsection (d)(1) and school corporation's determination
37 under subsection (d)(2) are correct. The department of local
38 government finance shall, not later than thirty (30) business days
39 after the date the department receives the school corporation's
40 request, make its determination and, if correct, certify that the
41 percentage computed under subsection (d)(1) and the
42 determination under subsection (d)(2) for the school corporation
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

29
1 meets the definition of an eligible school corporation.
2 (g) A school corporation that desires to be an eligible school
3 corporation under this section must, and notwithstanding any
4 other law, adopt a resolution that sets forth the following:
5 (1) The determinations made under subsection (d)(1) and
6 (d)(2), including the department of local government finance's
7 certification of the percentage computed under subsection (e)
8 and determination under subsection (d)(2).
9 (2) A determination providing for the:
10 (A) issuance of bonds to refund bonds or leases issued by
11 or on behalf of the school corporation before July 1, 2025;
12 and
13 (B) payment of redemption premiums and the costs of the
14 refunding.
15 (3) With respect to the refunding bonds, the following:
16 (A) The maximum principal amount.
17 (B) The maximum interest rate.
18 (C) The annual lease or debt service payment.
19 (D) The final maturity date.
20 (E) The estimated amount of the increment that will occur
21 for each year that the bonds that are being retired or
22 refunded by the issuance of refunding bonds would have
23 been outstanding.
24 (F) A finding that the annual debt service or lease payment
25 on the refunding bonds will not increase the annual debt
26 service or lease payment above the annual debt service or
27 lease payment approved by the school corporation for the
28 original project.
29 If the governing body adopts a resolution under this section, the
30 governing body must publish notice of the adoption of the
31 resolution as required by IC 5-3-1.
32 (h) An eligible school corporation may issue refunding bonds as
33 permitted by this section. In addition, an eligible school
34 corporation may extend the repayment period beyond the
35 repayment period for the bonds that are being retired or refunded
36 by the issuance of refunding bonds. However, the repayment
37 period may be extended only once for a particular bond, and the
38 extension may not exceed ten (10) years after the latest maturity
39 date for any of the bonds being retired or refunded by the eligible
40 school corporation under this section.

10 SECTION 19. IC 5-1-14-19 IS ADDED TO THE INDIANA CODE
11 AS A NEW SECTION TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE JULY
12 1, 2026]: Sec. 19. (a) This section applies to a contract between a
13 municipal entity and a municipal advisor entered into, renewed, or
14 amended after June 30, 2026.
15 (b) As used in this section, "municipal advisor" means an entity
16 defined as a municipal advisor under Section 15B of the Securities
17 Exchange Act.
18 (c) A municipal advisor must meet the following criteria:
19 (1) A municipal advisor employing more than one (1)
20 employee must employ at least one (1) individual who meets
21 both of the following criteria:
22 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
23 under applicable federal law and regulations.
24 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
25 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
26 54), or any successor examination recognized by the
27 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
28 (2) A municipal advisor employing only one (1) employee
29 must employ an individual who meets both of the following
30 criteria:
31 (A) The individual is registered as a municipal advisor
32 under applicable federal law and regulations.
33 (B) The individual has successfully passed the Municipal
34 Advisor Representative Qualification Examination (Series
35 50), or any successor examination recognized by the
36 Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board.
37 (d) As used in this section, "municipal entity" refers to:
38 (1) a state agency (as defined in IC 4-1-10-2);
39 (2) a county;
40 (3) a township;
41 (4) a city;
42 (5) a town;
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

33
1 (6) a school corporation;
2 (7) a special taxing district;
3 (8) an instrumentality of an entity listed in subdivisions (1)
4 through (7); and
5 (9) any other entity required to sell bonds pursuant to
6 IC 5-1-11.
7 (e) An entity that provides municipal advisory services,
8 including but not limited to financial planning, debt issuance, and
9 budget management advisory services, to:
10 (1) a state agency;
11 (2) a political subdivision (as defined in IC 36-1-2-13); or
12 (3) a municipal entity located in Indiana;
13 must comply with the municipal advisor qualification requirements
14 under subsection (c), starting January 1, 2027. An employee of a
15 state agency or employee of a political subdivision (as defined in
16 IC 36-1-2-13) is not required to meet the qualifications under
17 subsection (c).
18 (f) If a municipal entity hires or retains a municipal advisor, the
19 municipal entity shall complete a request for qualifications at least
20 once every three (3) years to select the municipal advisor. The
21 request for qualifications must include a scope of services and an
22 evaluation criteria outline. The request for qualifications shall be
23 completed before July 1 each year it is completed.
24 (g) Municipal entity contracts executed on or before July 1,
25 2023, with a term of three (3) years or more, shall expire on the
26 earlier of the contract expiration date or January 1, 2027.
27 (h) The municipal entity shall publish a contract entered into
28 with a municipal advisor in a prominent location on the municipal
29 entity's website and on the department of local government
30 finance's computer gateway.
31 (i) Once selected for a municipal entity's contract, the municipal
32 advisor shall register with the department of local government
33 finance. The department of local government finance shall give the
34 municipal advisor access to the municipal entity's gateway portal
35 upon receipt of their registration. If the municipal advisor does not
36 register with the department of local government finance, the
37 department of local government finance may issue a fine of ten
38 thousand dollars ($10,000) to the municipal advisor.

34 per square for the first insertion in newspapers or qualified

36
1 notice in a newspaper, or qualified publication may not exceed the

6 newspaper or qualified publication may, effective January 1 of
7 any year, increase the basic charges by not more than two and

37
1 publishing public notice advertising shall submit proof of publication

33 SECTION 25. IC 5-3-1-4 IS REPEALED [EFFECTIVE JULY 1,
34 2026]. Sec. 4. (a) Whenever officers of a political subdivision are
35 required to publish a notice affecting the political subdivision, they

37 subdivision.
38 (b) This subsection applies to notices published by county officers.

40 publication in that newspaper alone is sufficient.
41 (c) This subsection applies to notices published by city, town, or
42 school corporation officers. If there is only one (1) newspaper
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

42
1 published in the municipality or school corporation, then publication
2 in that newspaper alone is sufficient. If no newspaper is published in
3 the municipality or school corporation, then publication of the notice
4 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
5 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
6 school corporation.
7 (2) A newspaper published in the county in which the
8 municipality or school corporation is located and that circulates
9 within the municipality or school corporation.
10 (d) This subsection applies to notices published by officers of
11 political subdivisions not covered by subsection (a) or (b). If there is
12 only one (1) newspaper published in the political subdivision, then the
13 notice shall be published in that newspaper. If no newspaper is
14 published in the political subdivision, then publication of the notice
15 shall be made in one (1) of the following:
16 (1) A locality newspaper that circulates within the municipality or
17 school corporation.
18 (2) A newspaper published in the county and that circulates
19 within the political subdivision.
20 (e) This subsection applies to a political subdivision, including a
21 city, town, or school corporation. Notwithstanding any other law, if a
22 political subdivision has territory in more than one (1) county, public
23 notices that are required by law or ordered to be published must be
24 given as follows:
25 (1) By publication in two (2) newspapers published within the
26 boundaries of the political subdivision.
27 (2) If only one (1) newspaper is published within the boundaries
28 of the political subdivision, by publication of the notice in that
29 newspaper and in one (1) of the following:
30 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
31 subdivision.
32 (B) In another newspaper:
33 (i) published in any county in which the political subdivision
34 extends; and
35 (ii) that has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
36 (3) If no newspaper is published within the boundaries of the
37 political subdivision, by publishing the notice in two (2)
38 publications, consisting of either or both of the following:
39 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
40 subdivision.
41 (B) A newspaper that:
42 (i) is published in any counties into which the political
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

43
1 subdivision extends; and
2 (ii) has a general circulation in the political subdivision.
3 (4) If only one (1) newspaper is published in any of the counties
4 into which the political subdivision extends, by publication of the
5 notice in one (1) of the following:
6 (A) A locality newspaper that circulates within the political
7 subdivision.
8 (B) The newspaper published in the county if the newspaper
9 circulates within the political subdivision.
10 (f) A political subdivision may, in its discretion, publish public
11 notices in a qualified publication or additional newspapers or locality
12 newspapers to provide supplementary notification to the public. The
13 cost of publishing supplementary notification is a proper expenditure
14 of the political subdivision.

8 (2) IC 8-22-3.5 (airport development zones).

19 2025, 2026, and ending before January 1, 2034, adjust the base
20 assessed value of each tax increment financing allocation area to
21 neutralize the effect of the changing tax rates resulting year to year
22 from the homestead deduction under IC 6-1.1-12-37(c)(2) and
23 IC 6-1.1-12-37.5(c) and the deduction for eligible property under
24 IC 6-1.1-12-47. It is the intent of the general assembly that an increase
25 in revenue from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes
26 accrue only to the base assessed value and not to the tax increment
27 financing allocation area. However, in the case of a decrease in revenue
28 from a change in tax rates resulting from these statutes, the department
29 may neutralize the change under this subsection in a positive manner
30 with regard to the tax increment financing allocation area to protect the
31 ability to pay bonds based on incremental revenue, if the tax increment
32 financing allocation area demonstrates to the department that an
33 adjustment is needed before the department calculates a positive
34 neutralization adjustment.

35 year in which the assessment date occurs for which data is
36 available, before one (1) of those six (6) years is eliminated under
37 subdivision (3) when determining the rolling average.

42 the assessment date without applying the adjustment under this

48
1 subdivision, the department of local government finance shall

4 be at least ten percent (10%) greater than the final base rate
5 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization
6 rate of:
7 (i) for purposes of determining the preliminary base rate for
8 the January 1, 2025, and the January 1, 2026, and January
9 1, 2027, assessment dates, nine percent (9%); and

11 assessment dates before January 1, 2025, and for assessment
12 dates after December 31, 2026, 2027, eight percent (8%);
13 shall be used to determine the final base rate.
14 (B) If the preliminary base rate for the assessment date would
15 be at least ten percent (10%) less than the final base rate
16 determined for the preceding assessment date, a capitalization
17 rate of six percent (6%) shall be used to determine the final
18 base rate.

20 rate of seven percent (7%) shall be used to determine the final
21 base rate.

35 county assessor's proposed expenditure to carry out the appeal.
36 (3) The total amount of the proposed expenditure is in accordance
37 with an appropriation made by the county fiscal body in the
38 manner provided by law.

EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

55
1 section is a final unappealable order of the department of local
2 government finance.
3 (d) With respect to an appeal filed by a county assessor under this
4 section the following apply:
5 (1) In the petition for review to the Indiana board, the county
6 assessor shall state what the county assessor contends the
7 assessed value of the industrial facility should be and provide
8 substantial evidence in support of that contention. Failure to
9 comply with this requirement results in dismissal of the county
10 assessor's petition for review and no further appeal of the
11 assessment by the county assessor may be taken.
12 (2) Not later than thirty (30) days after the county assessor files a
13 petition for review in compliance with subdivision (1), the
14 Indiana board shall hold a hearing at which the county assessor
15 must establish a reasonable likelihood of success on any
16 contentions made in the petition for review including, without
17 limitation, the contention required under subdivision (1)
18 regarding the assessed value of the real estate. The industrial
19 company whose industrial facility is the subject of the county
20 assessor's petition for review and the department of local
21 government finance has the right to appear at this hearing and to
22 present testimony, to cross-examine witnesses, and to present
23 evidence regarding the county assessor's contentions.
24 (3) Not later than thirty (30) days after the hearing held under
25 subdivision (2), the Indiana board shall issue a determination
26 whether the county assessor has established a reasonable
27 likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition for review.
28 If the Indiana board determines that the county assessor has not
29 established a reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions
30 in the petition for review, the county assessor's petition for review
31 shall be dismissed and no further appeal of the assessment by the
32 county assessor may be taken. If the Indiana board determines that
33 the county assessor has established a reasonable likelihood of
34 success on the contentions in the petition for review, the Indiana
35 board's determination does not create the presumption that the
36 county assessor's contentions are valid. A determination by the
37 Indiana board that the county assessor has established a
38 reasonable likelihood of success on the contentions in the petition
39 for review may be appealed to the Indiana tax court as an
40 interlocutory appeal. A party may petition for review by the
41 Indiana supreme court of the Indiana tax court's ruling regarding
42 an interlocutory appeal brought under this subdivision.

42 thousand dollars ($500,000) until the fund reaches three million

1 dollars ($3,000,000).

16 SECTION 54. IC 6-1.1-12-14.5, AS AMENDED BY P.L.230-2025,
17 SECTION 33, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE
18 JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 14.5. (a) As used in this
19 section, "homestead" has the meaning set forth in section 37 of this
20 chapter.
21 (b) An individual may claim a deduction from the assessed value of
22 the individual's homestead if:
23 (1) the individual served in the military or naval forces of the
24 United States for at least ninety (90) days;
25 (2) the individual received an honorable discharge;
26 (3) the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%);
27 (4) the individual's disability is evidenced by:
28 (A) a pension certificate or an award of compensation issued
29 by the United States Department of Veterans Affairs; or
30 (B) a certificate of eligibility issued to the individual by the
31 Indiana department of veterans' affairs after the Indiana
32 department of veterans' affairs has determined that the
33 individual's disability qualifies the individual to receive a
34 deduction under this section; and
35 (5) the homestead was conveyed without charge to the individual
36 who is the owner of the homestead by an organization that is
37 exempt from income taxation under the federal Internal Revenue
38 Code.
39 (c) If an individual is entitled to a deduction from assessed value
40 under subsection (b) for the individual's homestead, the amount of the
41 deduction is determined as follows:
42 (1) If the individual is totally disabled, the deduction is equal to
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

66
1 one hundred percent (100%) of the assessed value of the
2 homestead.
3 (2) If the individual has a disability of at least ninety percent
4 (90%) but the individual is not totally disabled, the deduction is
5 equal to ninety percent (90%) of the assessed value of the
6 homestead.
7 (3) If the individual has a disability of at least eighty percent
8 (80%) but less than ninety percent (90%), the deduction is equal
9 to eighty percent (80%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
10 (4) If the individual has a disability of at least seventy percent
11 (70%) but less than eighty percent (80%), the deduction is equal
12 to seventy percent (70%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
13 (5) If the individual has a disability of at least sixty percent (60%)
14 but less than seventy percent (70%), the deduction is equal to
15 sixty percent (60%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
16 (6) If the individual has a disability of at least fifty percent (50%)
17 but less than sixty percent (60%), the deduction is equal to fifty
18 percent (50%) of the assessed value of the homestead.
19 (d) An individual who claims a deduction under this section for an
20 assessment date may not also claim a deduction under section 13
21 (before its expiration) or 14 of this chapter for that same assessment
22 date.
23 (e) An individual who desires to claim the deduction under this
24 section must claim the deduction in the manner specified by the
25 department of local government finance.

12 proving that the individual's residence is the individual's principal place
13 of residence as claimed in the certified statement filed under subsection
14 (e). The county auditor may limit the evidence that an individual is
15 required to submit to a state income tax return, a valid driver's license,
16 or a valid voter registration card showing that the residence for which
17 the deduction is claimed is the individual's principal place of residence.
18 The county auditor may not deny an application filed under section 44
19 of this chapter because the applicant does not have a valid driver's
20 license or state identification card with the address of the homestead
21 property. The department of local government finance shall work with
22 county auditors to develop procedures to determine whether a property
23 owner that is claiming a standard deduction or homestead credit is not
24 eligible for the standard deduction or homestead credit because the
25 property owner's principal place of residence is outside Indiana.
26 (l) A county auditor shall grant an individual a deduction under this
27 section regardless of whether the individual and the individual's spouse
28 claim a deduction on two (2) different applications and each
29 application claims a deduction for different property if the property
30 owned by the individual's spouse is located outside Indiana and the
31 individual files an affidavit with the county auditor containing the
32 following information:
33 (1) The names of the county and state in which the individual's
34 spouse claims a deduction substantially similar to the deduction
35 allowed by this section.
36 (2) A statement made under penalty of perjury that the following
37 are true:
38 (A) That the individual and the individual's spouse maintain
39 separate principal places of residence.
40 (B) That neither the individual nor the individual's spouse has
41 an ownership interest in the other's principal place of
42 residence.
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

79
1 (C) That neither the individual nor the individual's spouse has,
2 for that same year, claimed a standard or substantially similar
3 deduction for any property other than the property maintained
4 as a principal place of residence by the respective individuals.
5 A county auditor may require an individual or an individual's spouse to
6 provide evidence of the accuracy of the information contained in an
7 affidavit submitted under this subsection. The evidence required of the
8 individual or the individual's spouse may include state income tax
9 returns, excise tax payment information, property tax payment
10 information, driver's license information, and voter registration
11 information.
12 (m) If:
13 (1) a property owner files a statement under subsection (e) to
14 claim the deduction provided by this section for a particular
15 property; and
16 (2) the county auditor receiving the filed statement determines

18 the county auditor shall inform the property owner of the county

20 eligible for the deduction because the county auditor has determined

22 residence, the property owner may appeal the county auditor's
23 determination as provided in IC 6-1.1-15. The county auditor shall
24 inform the property owner of the owner's right to appeal when the
25 county auditor informs the property owner of the county auditor's
26 determination under this subsection.
27 (n) An individual is entitled to the deduction under this section for
28 a homestead for a particular assessment date if:
29 (1) either:
30 (A) the individual's interest in the homestead as described in
31 subsection (a)(2)(B) is conveyed to the individual after the
32 assessment date, but within the calendar year in which the
33 assessment date occurs; or
34 (B) the individual contracts to purchase the homestead after
35 the assessment date, but within the calendar year in which the
36 assessment date occurs;
37 (2) on the assessment date:
38 (A) the property on which the homestead is currently located
39 was vacant land; or
40 (B) the construction of the dwelling that constitutes the
41 homestead was not completed; and
42 (3) either:

80
1 (A) the individual files the certified statement required by
2 subsection (e); or
3 (B) a sales disclosure form that meets the requirements of
4 section 44 of this chapter is submitted to the county assessor
5 on or before December 31 of the calendar year for the
6 individual's purchase of the homestead.
7 An individual who satisfies the requirements of subdivisions (1)
8 through (3) is entitled to the deduction under this section for the
9 homestead for the assessment date, even if on the assessment date the
10 property on which the homestead is currently located was vacant land
11 or the construction of the dwelling that constitutes the homestead was
12 not completed. The county auditor shall apply the deduction for the

14 the homestead remains eligible for the deduction. A homestead that

31 subsection (a), then the county auditor shall provide electronic

34 political subdivision in the county subject to section 16 of this
35 chapter. The electronic notice must include a written statement

37 statutory deadline set forth in subsection (a) was not met.

39 February 2, 2027, and before February 2 of each year thereafter,

41 deadline set forth in subsection (a) to the legislative services agency
42 for distribution to the members of the legislative council. The

7 For purposes of this subdivision, "deficit" means a negative fund

10 calculation must be presented on an accrual basis according to
11 generally accepted accounting principles.

24 (8), and in the case of a school corporation, the board may also
25 designate a school corporation as a distressed political
26 subdivision if at least one (1) of the following conditions applies:
27 (A) The school corporation has:
28 (i) issued refunding bonds under IC 5-1-5-2.5 or
29 IC 5-1-5-2.6; or
30 (ii) adopted a resolution under IC 5-1-5-2.5 or IC 5-1-5-2.6;
31 making the determinations and including the information
32 specified in IC 5-1-5-2.5(g) and IC 5-1-5-2.6(d) and
33 IC 5-1-5-2.6(e).
34 (B) The ratio that the amount of the school corporation's debt
35 (as determined in December 2010) bears to the school
36 corporation's 2011 ADM ranks in the highest ten (10) among
37 all school corporations.
38 (C) The ratio that the amount of the school corporation's debt
39 (as determined in December 2010) bears to the school
40 corporation's total assessed valuation for calendar year 2011
41 ranks in the highest ten (10) among all school corporations.
42 (D) The amount of homestead assessed valuation in the school
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

103
1 corporation for calendar year 2011 was at least sixty percent
2 (60%) of the total amount of assessed valuation in the school
3 corporation for calendar year 2011.
4 The board may consider whether a political subdivision has fully
5 exercised all the local options available to the political subdivision,
6 such as a local option income tax or a local option income tax rate
7 increase or, in the case of a school corporation, an operating
8 referendum.
9 (b) If the board designates a political subdivision as distressed under
10 subsection (a), the board shall review the designation annually to
11 determine if the distressed political subdivision meets at least one (1)
12 of the conditions listed in subsection (a).
13 (c) If the board designates a political subdivision as a distressed
14 political subdivision under subsection (a), the board shall immediately
15 notify:
16 (1) the treasurer of state;
17 (2) the county auditor and county treasurer of each county in
18 which the distressed political subdivision is wholly or partially
19 located; and
20 (3) in the case of a school corporation, the Indiana education
21 employment relations board established by IC 20-29-3-1;
22 that the board has designated the political subdivision as a distressed
23 political subdivision.

37 SECTION 83. IC 6-1.1-20.6-9.8, AS AMENDED BY P.L.9-2024,
38 SECTION 171, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS
39 [EFFECTIVE JANUARY 1, 2026 (RETROACTIVE)]: Sec. 9.8. (a)
40 This section applies to property taxes first due and payable after
41 December 31, 2009.
42 (b) The following definitions apply throughout this section:
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

104
1 (1) "Debt service obligations of a political subdivision" refers to:
2 (A) the principal and interest payable during a calendar year
3 on bonds; and
4 (B) lease rental payments payable during a calendar year on
5 leases;
6 of a political subdivision payable from ad valorem property taxes.
7 (2) "Protected taxes" refers to the following:
8 (A) Property taxes that are exempted from the application of
9 a credit granted under section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by
10 section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or 7.5(c) of this chapter or another
11 law.
12 (B) Property taxes imposed by a political subdivision to pay
13 for debt service obligations of a political subdivision that are
14 not exempted from the application of a credit granted under
15 section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or
16 7.5(c) of this chapter or any other law. Property taxes
17 described in this clause are subject to the credit granted under
18 section 7 or 7.5 of this chapter by section 7(b), 7(c), 7.5(b), or
19 7.5(c) of this chapter regardless of their designation as
20 protected taxes.
21 (3) "Unprotected taxes" refers to property taxes that are not
22 protected taxes.
23 (c) Except as provided in section 9.9 of this chapter, the total
24 amount of revenue to be distributed to the fund for which the protected
25 taxes were imposed shall be determined as if no credit were granted
26 under section 7, or 7.5, or 7.7 of this chapter or under IC 6-1.1-49.
27 The total amount of the loss in revenue resulting from the granting of
28 credits under section 7, or 7.5, or 7.7 of this chapter or under
29 IC 6-1.1-49 must reduce only the amount of unprotected taxes
30 distributed to a fund using the following criteria:
31 (1) The reduction may be allocated in the amounts determined by
32 the political subdivision using a combination of unprotected taxes
33 of the political subdivision in those taxing districts in which the
34 credit caused a reduction in protected taxes.
35 (2) The tax revenue and each fund of any other political
36 subdivisions must not be affected by the reduction.
37 (d) When:
38 (1) the revenue that otherwise would be distributed to a fund
39 receiving only unprotected taxes is reduced entirely under
40 subsection (c) and the remaining revenue is insufficient for a fund
41 receiving protected taxes to receive the revenue specified by
42 subsection (c); or
EH 1210—LS 6805/DI 134

105
1 (2) there is not a fund receiving only unprotected taxes from
2 which to distribute revenue;
3 the revenue distributed to the fund receiving protected taxes must also
4 be reduced. If the revenue distributed to a fund receiving protected
5 taxes is reduced, the political subdivision may transfer money from one
6 (1) or more of the other funds of the political subdivision to offset the
7 loss in revenue to the fund receiving protected taxes. The transfer is
8 limited to the amount necessary for the fund receiving protected taxes
9 to receive the revenue specified under subsection (c). The amount
10 transferred shall be specifically identified as a debt service obligation
11 transfer for each affected fund.
12 SECTION 84. IC 6-1.1-20.6-9.9, AS AMENDED BY P.L.236-2023,
13 SECTION 39, IS AMENDED TO READ AS FOLLOWS [EFFECTIVE

15 applies to credits allocated before January 1, 2024. If:
16 (1) a school corporation after July 1, 2016, issues new bonds or
17 enters into a new lease rental agreement for which the school
18 corporation is imposing or will impose a debt service levy other
19 than:
20 (A) to refinance or renew prior bond or lease rental obligations
21 existing before January 1, 2017; or
22 (B) indebtedness that is approved in a local public question or
23 referendum under IC 6-1.1-20 or any other law; and
24 (2) the school corporation's:
25 (A) total debt service levy is greater than the school
26 corporation's total debt service levy in 2016; and
27 (B) total debt service tax rate is greater than the school

29 the school corporation is not eligible to allocate credits proportionately
